audits.area: Economic / feasibility model (cad/cost_benefit.py + scoring in cad/virtual_triage.py)
audits.summary: The feasibility model is a transparent deterministic spreadsheet, not a predictive model. Its clinical-phase approval priors (cad/cost_benefit.py:38-41) and clinical-phase costs (cad/cost_benefit.py:47-49) are genuinely and correctly transcribed from the cited BIO/Informa CDSR 2011-2020 and DiMasi 2016 figures — that part is real and well-attributed. But the surrounding machinery — the preclinical LOA, the "filed" cost, modality multipliers, the oncology factor, gross margin, and every verdict threshold — are invented heuristics presented inside the same output block under a single "sources" line that implies provenance they do not have. The "probability_of_approval" is literally a static modality/phase/oncology lookup multiplied together; it carries zero target-specific or molecule-specific signal, yet it is emitted per-"proposal" and labeled a feasibility verdict, inviting a target-specific reading. The benefit side mislabels risk-adjusted gross profit as "revenue," applies probability weighting to revenue but not to cost (asymmetric risk treatment), and uses no discounting over a ~9.5-year horizon, all of which inflate the benefit/cost ratio. The triage score weights are explicitly flagged non-validated, which is honest, but QED and Ro5 are combined in a way that double-counts Lipinski-space.
whatIsReal: Clinical-phase LOA priors are accurately transcribed from BIO/Informa CDSR 2011-2020: phase1 0.079, phase2 0.151, phase3 0.460, filed 0.906 (cad/cost_benefit.py:38-41) match the published all-disease phase-to-approval success rates (7.9% / 15.1% / ~46% / 90.6%).
Clinical per-phase out-of-pocket costs are accurately transcribed from DiMasi et al. 2016: phase1 $25M, phase2 $60M, phase3 $255M (cad/cost_benefit.py:47-49) match DiMasi's $25.3M / $58.6M / $255.4M.

The arithmetic itself is simple and correct: remaining cost = sum of forward per-phase costs x modality multiplier (cad/cost_benefit.py:80-81); peak revenue = incidence x price x penetration (cad/cost_benefit.py:85); BCR = risk-adjusted profit / cost (cad/cost_benefit.py:90).

The code is unusually candid in-line: it flags MODALITY_MULT as 'HEURISTIC, not fetched data' (cad/cost_benefit.py:54-55), the oncology factor as an 'editable, transparent adjustment' (cad/cost_benefit.py:66-68), and prints every assumption alongside the result (cad/cost_benefit.py:112-122, 143-147) with a 'not financial advice, not a valuation, not a clinical claim' disclaimer (cad/cost_benefit.py:146-147).

The triage ranking weights are explicitly labeled a 'TRANSPARENT, NON-VALIDATED ranking HEURISTIC ... not fitted to any benchmark ... never a prediction of efficacy' (cad/virtual_triage.py:228-232), and the underlying inputs (pChEMBL, QED, Ro5 violations) are real ChEMBL-computed values.
whatIsStubOrWrapper: Preclinical LOA 0.060 (cad/cost_benefit.py:37) is an invented round number — BIO CDSR is a clinical-phase (Phase I onward) report and contains no preclinical-to-approval rate; the comment '(overall, BIO 2011-2020)' on line 35 implies a source it does not have.

Modality multipliers (cad/cost_benefit.py:56-63) are hand-set constants applied to cost only — not to LOA and not to time — so e.g. CAR-T gets 1.8x cost but the identical approval probability and timeline as a small molecule.

ONCOLOGY_LOA_FACTOR 0.65 (cad/cost_benefit.py:68) is a single flat multiplier applied across all phases (cad/cost_benefit.py:76); it is not a sourced constant and ignores that the oncology/all-area LOA ratio varies sharply by phase in the BIO data.
gross_margin 0.80 (cad/cost_benefit.py:86) is a flat heuristic applied to all modalities including small molecules and cell/gene therapies whose real COGS differ greatly.

'filed' cost $20M and all per-phase durations (cad/cost_benefit.py:45-51) are rough planning estimates, not the DiMasi/Wong figures; DiMasi's preclinical out-of-pocket cost is far larger than the $10M on line 46.
Verdict thresholds 5 / 1.5 / 0.8 (cad/cost_benefit.py:92-99) are arbitrary cutoffs with no cited basis.

The entire 'feasibility verdict' is target- and molecule-agnostic: probability_of_approval (cad/cost_benefit.py:76) depends only on phase, modality flag, and oncology flag, so any two programs at the same phase/modality receive an identical verdict.

inaccuracies.title: Preclinical approval prior attributed to BIO but not in BIO report
inaccuracies.claim: LOA_FROM_PHASE is documented as 'overall, BIO 2011-2020' (cad/cost_benefit.py:35) and the assumptions output cites 'BIO/Informa CDSR 2011-2020' as the source (cad/cost_benefit.py:121).
inaccuracies.reality: The BIO/Informa/QLS Clinical Development Success Rates report only covers clinical phases (Phase I -> approval onward). It publishes no preclinical-to-approval rate. The 0.060 preclinical value (cad/cost_benefit.py:37) is an invented figure; its own comment hedges 'rough: ~6%' but it is still surfaced under the BIO citation.

inaccuracies.title: Risk-adjusted gross profit mislabeled as 'revenue'
inaccuracies.claim: Output field 'risk_adjusted_revenue_musd' (cad/cost_benefit.py:109) and the printed 'Risk-adjusted revenue' line (cad/cost_benefit.py:139) present the number as revenue.
inaccuracies.reality: risk_adj_rev = loa * peak_annual_rev * years_at_peak * gross_margin (cad/cost_benefit.py:87) multiplies by gross_margin 0.80, so it is a risk-adjusted gross-profit figure, not revenue. The BCR therefore compares profit to cost while the field name says revenue, which misleads a reader about what is being divided.

inaccuracies.title: No time-value discounting over a ~9.5-year horizon inflates the benefit/cost ratio
inaccuracies.claim: The model produces a 'risk-adjusted return' and benefit/cost ratio (cad/cost_benefit.py:87-90) used to drive a go/no-go verdict.
inaccuracies.reality: Peak-sales profit is counted undiscounted (cad/cost_benefit.py:87) even though the summed phase durations from preclinical are 1.5+1.5+2.5+3.0+1.0 = 9.5 years before any sales (cad/cost_benefit.py:45-51), and peak sales occur later still. With no NPV/discount rate, distant revenue is valued at face, materially overstating BCR vs. any real rNPV. There is no discount-rate parameter anywhere in the file.

inaccuracies.title: Asymmetric risk treatment: revenue is probability-weighted, cost is not
inaccuracies.claim: The model frames itself as an expected/risk-adjusted return calculation.
inaccuracies.reality: Revenue is multiplied by LOA (cad/cost_benefit.py:87) but remaining cost is the full deterministic forward path with no probability weighting (cad/cost_benefit.py:80-81). A proper expected-value/rNPV weights later-phase costs by the probability of reaching them; here costs are neither probability-weighted nor discounted, so cost and benefit are risk-adjusted on inconsistent bases, biasing the BCR.

inaccuracies.title: Wong et al. 2019 cited for per-phase cost it does not provide
inaccuracies.claim: 'Per-phase out-of-pocket cost & duration: Wong, Siah & Lo (2019) and DiMasi et al. (2016)' (cad/cost_benefit.py:19-20); sources line repeats 'Wong et al. 2019' (cad/cost_benefit.py:121).
inaccuracies.reality: The per-phase out-of-pocket cost numbers (25/60/255) trace to DiMasi 2016, not Wong et al. 2019, whose contribution is success-rate and duration estimation. Wong's headline Phase I PoS (~13.8%, 2000-2015) also differs from the BIO 7.9% actually used (cad/cost_benefit.py:38), so Wong is cited but its numbers are not the ones encoded.

inaccuracies.title: Verdict reads as a target/program-specific prediction but is a pure modality x phase benchmark
inaccuracies.claim: Header calls it a per-proposal 'feasibility verdict' / 'probability of regulatory approval' for a proposed therapeutic system (cad/cost_benefit.py:1-10), and the verdict string asserts a program-level conclusion (cad/cost_benefit.py:92-99).
inaccuracies.reality: probability_of_approval is just LOA_FROM_PHASE[phase] x oncology factor (cad/cost_benefit.py:76); the verdict adds only user-entered market inputs. No biology, target, or molecule enters the calculation, so every program at the same phase/modality/oncology flag gets the same approval probability and the same verdict — it is a benchmark, not a prediction, despite per-proposal presentation.

inaccuracies.title: Verdict BCR thresholds have no cited or derived basis
inaccuracies.claim: BCR >= 5 'Strongly favorable', >=1.5 'Favorable', >=0.8 'Marginal', else 'Unfavorable' (cad/cost_benefit.py:92-99).
inaccuracies.reality: These cutoffs are arbitrary round numbers with no source or sensitivity analysis; combined with the undiscounted, profit-vs-cost BCR they determine the headline go/no-go label, giving false precision to an unvalidated boundary.

inaccuracies.title: Drug-likeness score double-counts Lipinski space (QED + Ro5)
inaccuracies.claim: drug_likeness_score = 0.6*QED + 0.4*Ro5-compliance, described as 'QED + Lipinski Ro5 compliance' (cad/virtual_triage.py:233-239).
inaccuracies.reality: QED (Bickerton et al.) already folds MW, ALOGP, HBD, HBA, PSA, rotatable bonds, aromatic rings and structural alerts into its desirability — it heavily overlaps the same Lipinski properties the separate 0.4*Ro5 term re-penalizes, so molecular-weight/logP/H-bond space is counted twice. The 0.34-per-violation penalty (cad/virtual_triage.py:238) and the pChEMBL/11 cap (cad/virtual_triage.py:244) are also arbitrary. The code does label the whole score non-validated (cad/virtual_triage.py:228-232).

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Useful today only as a transparent, editable order-of-magnitude planning calculator for a portfolio/BD analyst who already understands rNPV and treats the output as a sanity-check sketch. It correctly reproduces published BIO phase success rates and DiMasi phase costs, so it can ballpark 'is this program in the right zip code.' It would mislead anyone who reads probability_of_approval as a forecast for their specific target or molecule (it is identical for all programs at a given phase/modality), who treats the BCR as a valuation (no discounting, profit mislabeled as revenue, cost not risk-weighted), or who trusts the preclinical 6% and the favorable/unfavorable thresholds as sourced (they are invented). It is not usable by oncologists or for any clinical decision, and med-chemists should note the triage drug-likeness score double-weights Lipinski properties. The honest in-code disclaimers help, but the single 'sources' line on the output blends sourced and invented numbers without distinction.
audits.readmeNotes: Any public limitations section should state plainly: (1) Only the clinical-phase LOAs (Phase I/II/III/Filed, cost_benefit.py:38-41) and clinical-phase costs (cost_benefit.py:47-49) come from the cited BIO and DiMasi sources; the preclinical approval rate, 'filed' cost, durations, modality multipliers, oncology factor, gross margin, and all verdict thresholds are unsourced heuristics. The 'sources: BIO/Informa CDSR 2011-2020; Wong et al. 2019; DiMasi et al. 2016' string (cost_benefit.py:121) should not be presented as covering the whole assumption block. (2) probability_of_approval is a modality/phase benchmark identical for every program at that phase — it is NOT a target- or molecule-specific prediction; the word 'verdict' overclaims. (3) The benefit/cost ratio is undiscounted (no NPV over a ~9.5-year horizon), risk-weights revenue but not cost, and the 'risk-adjusted revenue' field is actually gross profit (x0.80 margin) — so the ratio is not a valuation. (4) The triage drug-likeness score combines QED and Ro5, which overlap, so Lipinski properties are partly double-counted; weights are non-validated (already disclosed at virtual_triage.py:228-232). Avoid any phrasing like 'predicts approval probability' or 'expected return' without the benchmark/heuristic caveat.

audits.area: Core CADD pipeline (cad/run_pipeline.py, target_report.py, virtual_triage.py, binding_site.py, fetch_structure.py)
audits.summary: The pipeline is genuinely wired to live public APIs (ChEMBL, UniProt, RCSB, AlphaFold) and computes real things from real data — it is not a template generator, and it is unusually honest about its heuristics (scoring weights are explicitly flagged as non-validated). But the scientific core has several real CADD correctness problems. The docking box is the bounding envelope of whatever hetero group has the most atoms in a single PDB — not necessarily the co-crystal inhibitor — and its center (mean of atoms) and size (min/max extent) are computed inconsistently. The three resolution paths (ChEMBL target, UniProt entry, best PDB) are independent fuzzy lookups that can land on different proteins, and only virtual_triage does exact gene-symbol disambiguation; target_report and fetch_structure do not. The "potent activities" count applies no potency floor, and the ligand triage scans only an arbitrary, unordered first slice of ChEMBL activities while pooling IC50/Ki/Kd/EC50 and keeping the single most favorable value per molecule. Useful as cited research orientation; misleading if read as a validated potency/druggability ranking.
whatIsReal: Live, keyless data pulls from real public APIs with correct endpoints: ChEMBL activity/molecule/mechanism/target_search (virtual_triage.py:43, target_report.py:32), UniProt search with reviewed+human+gene/protein filters (target_report.py:79, fetch_structure.py:55), RCSB file download and AlphaFold API version resolution (fetch_structure.py:32-34, 76-95).
Real PDB ATOM/HETATM column parsing by fixed-width offsets for ligand coordinates (binding_site.py:57-76) — genuine geometry from the actual structure file, not a placeholder.

pChEMBL is correctly described as -log10(molar potency) and used as the potency axis (virtual_triage.py:8, 367); RDKit ECFP4/Morgan r=2 Tanimoto similarity is a real, standard fingerprint when RDKit is present (virtual_triage.py:251-266).

virtual_triage.resolve_target does real exact gene-symbol disambiguation to avoid ChEMBL text-relevance picking an interactor/substring (e.g. AKT1 vs AKT1S1, AURKA vs AURKAIP1) (virtual_triage.py:81-101) — a genuine, well-reasoned correctness guard.
AlphaFold model version is resolved live rather than hardcoded, with newest-first fallback (fetch_structure.py:76-95).

The scoring weights are explicitly and prominently labeled as a transparent non-validated heuristic, not fetched data (virtual_triage.py:228-232), and SUMMARY.md flags cost-benefit as target-independent (run_pipeline.py:241-243).
Pipeline refuses to emit a feasibility verdict when the target cannot be resolved, rather than fabricating one (run_pipeline.py:74-93).
whatIsStubOrWrapper: RDKit-dependent steps (similarity, PAINS/Brenk liabilities, SDF, extra descriptors) only run if RDKit is installed; otherwise silently skipped with a SUMMARY note (run_pipeline.py:101-109, 224-226; virtual_triage.py:47-54, 292-298).

Docking itself is not run — stage 6 (cad/dock.py / AutoDock Vina) is only a printed command string; the pipeline never validates the box against a real dock (run_pipeline.py:250-256, binding_site.py:105-107).

cost_benefit P(approval) and benefit/cost are target-independent constants from a fixed BIO/Informa prior, not computed from this target (run_pipeline.py:159-164); provenance source_url is called with entry=None (run_pipeline.py:161).
Box ligand selection has no relevance model — it is purely max-atom-count over hetero groups not in a hand-curated IGNORE set (binding_site.py:31-35, 74).

inaccuracies.title: Docking box ligand is the largest hetero group, not necessarily the co-crystal inhibitor
inaccuracies.claim: README and provenance call it the 'docking box from the co-crystal ligand' / 'geometric envelope of the co-crystal ligand' (run_pipeline.py:10, 156-157; README.md:118).
inaccuracies.reality: largest_ligand picks the single hetero residue with the most atoms (binding_site.py:74 `key = max(groups, key=lambda k: len(groups[k]))`). The IGNORE set (binding_site.py:31-35) covers water/ions/common cryo-additives but NOT glycans (NAG/MAN/BMA/FUC), lipids/detergents, peptides, nucleotides, or covalent cofactors (HEME/FAD/NAD). On a glycosylated or cofactor-bound structure the box can center on a sugar tree or cofactor, not the inhibitor pocket. There is also no check that this ligand corresponds to the pocket where the triaged hits would bind.

inaccuracies.title: Box center (mean of atoms) and box size (min/max extent) are computed on inconsistent bases
inaccuracies.claim: A center+size that encloses the ligand for AutoDock Vina (binding_site.py:8-9, 79-83).
inaccuracies.reality: center = arithmetic mean of atom coordinates (binding_site.py:81) while size = (max-min)+2*pad (binding_site.py:82). Vina interprets the box as center +/- size/2, i.e. center should be the bounding-box midpoint (max+min)/2, not the atom mean. When ligand atoms are unevenly distributed the mean is offset from the geometric center, so the box is not symmetric about the true extent; for ligands spanning >16 Angstrom on an axis the 8 Angstrom pad can fail to compensate and the box can clip the ligand. Center and size describe different reference points.

inaccuracies.title: 8 Angstrom per-side padding (16 Angstrom added per axis) is a hardcoded constant, large for focused re-docking
inaccuracies.claim: 'ligand envelope + 8 Angstrom padding' as a sensible Vina box (binding_site.py:122).
inaccuracies.reality: pad=8.0 is a fixed default (binding_site.py:79); the box adds 16 Angstrom to each axis on top of the full ligand extent. For a typical ~10 Angstrom small molecule this yields ~26 Angstrom boxes. Vina guidance favors tighter boxes for redocking (oversized boxes degrade search accuracy/exhaustiveness). The pad is a constant, not derived from pocket geometry or any pocket-detection (fpocket/P2Rank) the docstrings mention.

inaccuracies.title: 'Potent activities' count applies no potency threshold
inaccuracies.claim: Docstring: 'how many potent measured ligands exist' (target_report.py:11-12); README: 'EGFR: ChEMBL potent activities = 21342' (README.md:54); SUMMARY: 'Potent ChEMBL activities' (run_pipeline.py:179); provenance key 'chembl_potent_activity_records' (run_pipeline.py:143).
inaccuracies.reality: The query only requires pchembl_value to be non-null, with NO pchembl floor (target_report.py:61 `pchembl_value__isnull: false`). It counts every activity with any pChEMBL value, including very weak ones (pChEMBL 2 = 10 mM). It also counts activity RECORDS, not distinct ligands (a single compound contributes many records), contradicting 'how many potent measured ligands exist'. The word 'potent' is unjustified.

inaccuracies.title: Ligand triage scans an arbitrary unordered slice of ChEMBL, then calls it 'the most potent'
inaccuracies.claim: 'Pulls the experimentally measured potent ligands' and 'Take the most potent molecules' / ranked best candidates (virtual_triage.py:6-7, 286-288; run_pipeline.py:151 'ChEMBL bioactivities ranked by a transparent potency+drug-likeness score').
inaccuracies.reality: fetch_actives issues the activity query with NO order_by (virtual_triage.py:123-131), so ChEMBL returns its default order (activity_id ascending). It scans only the first `scan` (default 4000) records (virtual_triage.py:117, 382) and a wall-clock budget can cut it shorter (virtual_triage.py:116-121). For high-data targets (EGFR has >>4000 activities) the 'top' molecules are the best of an arbitrary early slice, not the globally most potent. Results are also non-deterministic under API slowness.

inaccuracies.title: Potency conflates assay types/relations and keeps the single most favorable value per molecule
inaccuracies.claim: 'IC50 / Ki / Kd / EC50, expressed as pChEMBL' treated as one comparable potency (virtual_triage.py:8, 45).
inaccuracies.reality: POTENCY_TYPES pools IC50 (assay-condition-dependent), Ki, Kd, and EC50 (cellular) into one axis (virtual_triage.py:45, 139), and per molecule keeps the MAXIMUM pChEMBL across all of them (virtual_triage.py:147-149) — cherry-picking the most potent assay, an optimistic bias. No filter on standard_relation (censored '>' values pass), assay_type (B vs F), data_validity_comment, or target/assay confidence. best_pchembl is a single best measurement, not an aggregate.

inaccuracies.title: Target resolution differs between stages; the three lookups can describe different proteins
inaccuracies.claim: A single coherent dossier+triage+structure for one target (run_pipeline.py manifest ties uniprot_accession, pdb_count, chembl activity, and docking box together as one target, run_pipeline.py:132-165).
inaccuracies.reality: target_report.resolve_target sorts only by (SINGLE PROTEIN, Homo sapiens) booleans with NO exact gene-symbol guard (target_report.py:48-56), unlike virtual_triage which does (virtual_triage.py:81-101) — so the dossier and the triaged ligands can resolve to DIFFERENT ChEMBL targets for the same input. Separately, uniprot_summary (target_report.py:78-81) and fetch_structure.resolve_uniprot (fetch_structure.py:53-56) take the first (size:1) gene-OR-protein_name match with no cross-check that the UniProt accession equals the ChEMBL target's protein. pdb_count, activity count, and the docking-box PDB can therefore belong to different proteins while being reported as one.

inaccuracies.title: Best-PDB selection optimizes resolution, ignoring whether a ligand is present (holo vs apo)
inaccuracies.claim: Pipeline produces a docking box whenever an experimental PDB exists (run_pipeline.py:114-120).
inaccuracies.reality: pick_best_pdb returns the lowest-resolution X-ray/EM entry (fetch_structure.py:98-105), which is frequently an apo structure. binding_site then reports 'no non-solvent ligand found (apo structure?)' and the box is silently skipped (binding_site.py:99-103), even when a holo structure of the same target exists. There is no search for a ligand-bound structure.

inaccuracies.title: 'docking feasible: yes' inferred from mere existence of any PDB
inaccuracies.claim: Dossier prints 'docking feasible: yes' when pdb_count>0 (target_report.py:174-176; run_pipeline.py:175).
inaccuracies.reality: pdb_count counts all UniProt PDB cross-references including apo, NMR, fragment, and partial-domain/complex structures (target_report.py:93, 99). Presence of any PDB does not establish that a dockable pocket structure exists. Overclaims docking feasibility.

inaccuracies.title: Composite/drug-likeness score constants look like meaningful quantities
inaccuracies.claim: A 'score' column ranking candidates (virtual_triage.py:242-248, CSV/table output).
inaccuracies.reality: potency is normalized by hardcoded constants min(pchembl,11)/11 (virtual_triage.py:244) and combined with weights 0.6/0.4 (or 0.45/0.3/0.25) and a 0.34-per-Ro5-violation penalty (virtual_triage.py:238, 247-248). These are arbitrary design constants, not fitted or validated. This is explicitly disclaimed in code (virtual_triage.py:228-232), so it is honest, but the numeric 'score' can still be read as predictive.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: For a researcher or med-chemist this is a legitimate, cited starting point: it pulls real ChEMBL ligands with SMILES and links, real UniProt/PDB metadata, and a real structure file, all keyless and reproducible. It is NOT safe to read as a validated ranking. A med-chemist would be misled by (a) 'potent activities = N' which has no potency floor and counts records not compounds; (b) the 'top candidates' which are best-of-an-arbitrary-4000-record slice (no order_by) with the single most favorable IC50/Ki/Kd/EC50 kept per molecule; and (c) the docking box, which can be built on a glycan/cofactor/peptide rather than the inhibitor and whose center/size are geometrically inconsistent — feeding it straight into Vina (as SUMMARY.md instructs) could dock into the wrong region or clip the pocket. The cross-stage resolution mismatch means the dossier, the ligands, and the structure are not guaranteed to be the same protein. No oncologist/clinical use; it is explicitly research orientation and should stay there.
audits.readmeNotes: README.md:118 'Docking box computed from the co-crystal ligand envelope' should be softened to 'largest non-solvent hetero ligand (may not be the inhibitor; verify pocket)'. README.md:54 'ChEMBL potent activities = 21342' is inaccurate — there is no potency threshold (only pchembl non-null) and it counts activity records, not potent ligands; rename to 'ChEMBL activities with a pChEMBL value'. README.md:157/191 imply the docking box is reliable for the intended pocket; add the caveat that the box derives from whichever PDB has best resolution (often apo) and from max-atom-count ligand selection, and that the box ligand is unrelated to the triaged hits. A 'Known limitations' doc should also state: target/UniProt/PDB are resolved by three independent fuzzy lookups that can disagree; ligand scan is an unordered first-N slice (not globally most potent); IC50/Ki/Kd/EC50 are pooled and the most favorable value per molecule is kept; the composite 'score' is an unvalidated heuristic of arbitrary constants.

audits.summary: The verification spine is real and better than typical "trust me" provenance: verify.py genuinely re-fetches ChEMBL/UniProt counts, does an independent raw-HTTP SMILES byte-equality pull, and deterministically recomputes cost_benefit, the docking box, PAINS/Brenk/SA liabilities, and the triage score, failing CI on mismatch. But the marketing ("every figure re-pulled from source", "fabrication impossible to hide", "independently re-verifiable") overstates it in five concrete ways: (1) most numeric columns in hits.csv (pChEMBL potency, QED, Ro5, MW, alogp, similarity) are NEVER re-fetched — the score recompute reads them straight from the same row it is checking, so an internally-consistent fabrication passes; (2) DRIFT silently blesses any count change up to max(2, 10%) and still prints "No fabrications"; (3) the SMILES check only proves the SMILES matches the chembl_id in the row, not that the molecule is actually a target hit, so a real-but-wrong compound passes; (4) wrong-but-reproducible queries pass by design, and "potent_activity_records" is itself a mislabeled query; (5) provenance.py's "model origin impossible" is just a string-enum check on a label — it never verifies the value actually came from the source, and verify.py never even reads provenance.json. So the system proves reproducibility/non-tampering of a subset of figures, not correctness, and not freedom from model influence.
whatIsReal: Independent SMILES identity check genuinely uses a separate code path: verify.py:56-72 does raw urllib GET against the ChEMBL molecule endpoint, distinct from virtual_triage.py:163's batched fetch_molecule_properties, and requires byte-equality (verify.py:167).
Deterministic cost_benefit recompute really catches tampering of the model OUTPUTS — test_provenance.py:52-63 proves a +99 edit to benefit_cost_ratio FAILs (verify.py:227-234).
Docking box is re-derived from the live PDB co-crystal ligand envelope and must match exactly (verify.py:259-267), a true recompute from source not a self-comparison.
PAINS/Brenk/GSK/Pfizer/SA liabilities are recomputed from saved SMILES via RDKit and must reproduce (verify.py:289-319).
Count checks re-issue the documented ChEMBL/UniProt queries through the stage modules (verify.py:108-132) and print an openable verify URL per figure (verify.py:403-404).
The empty-run guard is real: a run with no dossier/hits cannot earn the green banner and FAILs (verify.py:380-383, 408-411).
provenance.Figure rejects origin literals other than 'fetched'/'computed' (provenance.py:93-98), and test_provenance.py:24-30 enforces it.
The docstrings are unusually honest about scope, explicitly disclaiming prose, intel digests, and 'a wrong-but-stable query would pass' (verify.py:15-28).
whatIsStubOrWrapper: The triage score recompute is self-referential: verify.py:192-198 reads best_pchembl, qed, ro5_violations, and similarity from the SAME CSV row being verified, then feeds them to composite_score. It only catches editing the score column ALONE; none of those input numbers is ever re-fetched from ChEMBL.
similarity (Tanimoto) is never recomputed or re-derived — it is taken at face value from the CSV (verify.py:194) and also requires the original --query SMILES, which verify never has.
Per-row potency/property columns (best_pchembl, qed, ro5, mw, alogp, hba, hbd, tpsa) are NOT re-verified against ChEMBL at all; only the molecule's canonical_smiles is re-pulled.

cost_benefit verification recomputes ONLY from the saved assumptions block (verify.py:214-221) — the assumptions themselves (incidence, price, penetration, years) are unverified inputs and the phase-success priors inside cost_benefit.py are hardcoded, not fetched from the cited DOI.

provenance.json is write-only/decorative: verify.py never reads it (run() at verify.py:343-383 reads dossier/hits/liabilities/binding_site/cost_benefit, never provenance.json), so nothing cross-checks artifact figures against the manifest or the manifest's origin labels.

Manifest.computed sets verify_url to a formula filename like 'cad/virtual_triage.py:composite_score' (provenance.py:113), not an openable re-verification URL, yet the test only asserts it is truthy (test_provenance.py:41).

RDKit-absent and network-failure paths SKIP rather than FAIL: liabilities (verify.py:279-282), binding box on any fetch exception (verify.py:252-253), SMILES 'unresolved' rows silently skipped unless ALL fail (verify.py:177-178) — a mostly-SKIP run still avoids the FAIL banner.
Coverage caps: verify_hits checks only the first 5 rows (max_rows=5, verify.py:142) and liabilities only the first 25 (verify.py:270); rows beyond the cap are never verified.

inaccuracies.title: "Re-pulls every figure from its live source" — most hits.csv figures are not re-pulled
inaccuracies.claim: README:35/135 and verify.py:5-7 state it re-pulls EVERY reported figure from its live public source.
inaccuracies.reality: Only ChEMBL/UniProt counts and each ligand's canonical SMILES are re-fetched. The potency (best_pchembl), QED, Ro5, MW, alogp, HBA/HBD, TPSA and similarity columns in hits.csv are read from the file and never re-fetched; the score 'recompute' consumes those unverified row values as its inputs (verify.py:192-198).

inaccuracies.title: Self-consistent fabrication of potency passes the score check
inaccuracies.claim: Deterministic recompute means "any drift means the score column was edited" / fabrication impossible to hide (verify.py:182-184, README:7).
inaccuracies.reality: composite_score is recomputed from best_pchembl/qed/ro5/similarity taken from the same row. Editing best_pchembl AND score together (or fabricating a whole row consistently) reproduces and PASSES — only an isolated edit of the score column is caught.

inaccuracies.title: DRIFT silently blesses changed numbers and is reported as "No fabrications"
inaccuracies.claim: Marketing: fabrication impossible to hide; figures are re-verifiable.
inaccuracies.reality: _count_status returns DRIFT (non-failing, exit 0) for any change within max(2, 10%) and the banner prints "✅ No fabrications. N figure(s) DRIFTed". A fabricator nudging a count by up to 10% (or 2 absolute) is reported as no-fabrication; direction (a count going DOWN) is ignored.

inaccuracies.title: SMILES byte-equal proves ID↔SMILES consistency, not that the molecule is a target hit
inaccuracies.claim: README:35/144-145: byte-equal to ChEMBL catches an edited/fabricated ligand; "fabrication impossible to hide."
inaccuracies.reality: The check fetches canonical_smiles for the chembl_id IN THE ROW and compares (verify.py:164-167). Any real ChEMBL compound + its real SMILES passes regardless of whether it is actually a potent ligand of the target. It cannot detect a wrong-but-real molecule inserted into hits.csv.

inaccuracies.title: "byte-equal to ChEMBL" reads the same canonical_smiles field the pipeline read — not a fully orthogonal source
inaccuracies.claim: README:35 / verify.py:20-22 frame the SMILES check as genuinely independent.
inaccuracies.reality: The HTTP path is separate, but both verify (_chembl_canonical_smiles, verify.py:68-72) and the pipeline (virtual_triage.py:167-178) read the identical molecule_structures.canonical_smiles field from ChEMBL. It verifies the file faithfully copied that field; it is not an independent measurement of the molecule.

inaccuracies.title: "potent_activity_records" counts any pchembl-non-null activity, regardless of potency or assay type
inaccuracies.claim: The figure is labeled/reported as POTENT activity records (target_report.py:75, manifest 'chembl_potent_activity_records').
inaccuracies.reality: chembl_snapshot counts every activity with pchembl_value not null (target_report.py:61) — no min pChEMBL, no standard_type/POTENCY_TYPES filter, no assay-confidence filter. This includes weak (e.g. pChEMBL ~1) and non-binding functional records. The verifier re-runs the same query so it reproduces, giving false confidence in a mislabeled number. (verify.py:16-19 admits 'a wrong-but-stable query would pass.')

inaccuracies.title: provenance.py does NOT make a model origin impossible — it only checks a label string
inaccuracies.claim: README:34: "The code cannot record a value whose origin is 'model' — it raises." Core rule: no figure originates from a model.
inaccuracies.reality: Figure.__post_init__ only checks the origin STRING equals 'fetched'/'computed' (provenance.py:93-98). value is typed `object` and is never tied to the source; any code can call manifest.fetched('x', <model_produced_value>, 'chembl', plausible_url) and it is accepted. The invariant blocks only the literal honesty of writing origin='model'.

inaccuracies.title: Manifest is never verified against the artifacts it describes
inaccuracies.claim: README:34/120: provenance.json tags every reported figure with origin+source+verify URL as the anti-hallucination guarantee.
inaccuracies.reality: verify.py never opens provenance.json (run() at verify.py:343-383). Nothing checks that the figures/origins in the manifest match dossier.json/hits.csv, so the manifest could disagree with the artifacts and verify would still print a green banner.
inaccuracies.evidence: verify.py:343-383; provenance.json written at run_pipeline.py:165 but absent from verify's reads

inaccuracies.title: cost-benefit "reproduces exactly" only validates internal consistency, not the assumptions or priors
inaccuracies.claim: verify.py:235-237 prints "all figures reproduce exactly from stated assumptions"; README markets fully-cited figures.
inaccuracies.reality: verify_cost_benefit recomputes from the saved assumptions block (verify.py:214-221); the assumptions (incidence/price/penetration/years) are unverified inputs and the per-phase success priors are hardcoded in cost_benefit.py, not fetched from the cited DOI. Fabricated assumptions producing a self-consistent verdict PASS.

inaccuracies.title: SKIP/unresolved paths convert un-checkable figures into a non-FAIL
inaccuracies.claim: "No result is better than a fabricated one"; non-zero exit gates CI.
inaccuracies.reality: RDKit-absent liabilities (verify.py:279-282), any binding-box fetch error (verify.py:252-253), and partially-unresolvable SMILES (verify.py:177-178) all SKIP. Only ALL-rows-unresolved FAILs. An attacker who can make verify's network fetch fail (or simply lacks RDKit) downgrades real checks to SKIP and still avoids the FAIL banner.

inaccuracies.title: Only the first 5 hits (and 25 liabilities) are verified
inaccuracies.claim: Implied: the published ranked ligands are verified.
inaccuracies.reality: verify_hits reads only the first 5 CSV rows (verify.py:147, max_rows=5) and liabilities only the first 25 (verify.py:270). Any fabricated row past the cap is never checked.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: For its actual target users — med-chemists and computational researchers using this as an OSINT triage front-end — the verifier is genuinely useful as a tamper-detector and reproducibility gate: it will catch hand-edited SMILES, a doctored score column, an edited cost_benefit verdict, or a fabricated docking box, and it forces an openable URL next to every count. That is more than most pipelines offer. Where it would mislead: a reader who believes the README's "every figure re-pulled" / "fabrication impossible to hide" will over-trust the potency and drug-likeness numbers in hits.csv, which are taken on faith from the file (only SMILES and the derived score are checked, and the score is checked against those same unverified inputs). It would also mislead anyone treating a PASS as evidence the query is CORRECT — a mislabeled "potent_activity_records" count (no potency/assay filter) reproduces and passes. No clinician or patient should touch this, and the repo says so. Bottom line: this proves "these specific figures were not altered after the run and re-derive from the documented query," NOT "these figures are correct, well-defined, or model-free." The "no value originates from a model" guarantee is a labeling convention, not an enforced property.
audits.readmeNotes: Three lines overclaim relative to the code: README:7 "make fabrication impossible to hide" — false; DRIFT blesses ±10%/2 changes (verify.py:77-86,413), the score check is self-referential (verify.py:192-198), and rows past index 5/25 are unchecked. README:35 and README:135 "Re-pulls every figure from its live source" — only counts and SMILES are re-pulled; potency/QED/Ro5/MW/alogp/similarity are read from the file, not re-fetched. README:34 "The code cannot record a value whose origin is 'model' — it raises" — it only rejects the literal string 'model' on a label (provenance.py:93-98); a model-produced number passed to manifest.fetched(...) is accepted, and verify.py never reads provenance.json to cross-check. Suggested caveats: (a) state that verify proves non-tampering + reproducibility of a SUBSET of figures, not correctness; (b) note hits.csv property/potency columns and similarity are NOT independently re-fetched; (c) clarify DRIFT is a non-failing tolerance band and explain why "No fabrications" is printed for it; (d) rename or footnote "potent_activity_records" since the query applies no potency/assay filter; (e) drop or soften "fabrication impossible to hide" to "tampering with covered figures is detectable." docs/ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md (referenced at README:216) should carry the explicit threat model.

audits.summary: The cheminformatics layer is genuinely real, not a fake. It is a thin, faithful wrapper over RDKit: PAINS/Brenk use the real FilterCatalog, QED is RDKit's real Bickerton QED, descriptors (MW, MolLogP, TPSA, HBD/HBA, rotatable bonds, aromatic rings, FractionCSP3, Murcko scaffold) are real RDKit calls, ECFP4 Tanimoto and Butina clustering are real, and SA score is the real Ertl/Schuffenhauer sascorer imported from RDKit's Contrib tree (verified: aspirin SA=1.58, QED=0.550, PAINS descriptions like 'quinone_A(370)'/'catechol_A(92)' are genuine RDKit catalog strings). The drug-likeness rule sets (Ro5, Veber, Egan, GSK 4/400, Pfizer 3/75, LE, LLE) are implemented to commonly-cited spec with minor simplifications. The main fidelity caveats are: (a) 'clean' silently ignores Brenk alerts; (b) the Egan rule is a rectangular approximation of an ellipsoidal model; (c) the committed JSON is a live-ChEMBL snapshot (2026-06-23) whose compound set is not reproducible, and the precompute script's deliberate 'never shrink' guards can leave stale per-target files in place while index.json's date advances. The SA scorer degrades honestly to None/omitted field when Contrib is absent.
whatIsReal: PAINS and Brenk alerts use RDKit's real FilterCatalog (cheminformatics.py:61-70): FilterCatalogParams + AddCatalog(P.PAINS)/(P.BRENK). Verified against live RDKit 2026.03.2 — returns genuine catalog descriptions 'quinone_A(370)', 'catechol_A(92)' (PAINS) and 'chinone_1','catechol','imine_1','oxime_1' (Brenk), which match the committed JSON (e.g. BRAF.json brenk:['imine_1','oxime_1','Oxygen-nitrogen_single_bond']).
QED is the real RDKit QED.qed (cheminformatics.py:85) — verified aspirin qed=0.55 exactly matches direct QED.qed=0.5501. Not a homemade approximation.

Synthetic-accessibility is the real Ertl & Schuffenhauer 2009 sascorer imported from RDKit Contrib SA_Score (cheminformatics.py:46-56, 100). fpscores.pkl.gz is present in the pip rdkit wheel used by CI; verified aspirin SA=1.58 (sane low value).

All physicochemical descriptors are real RDKit: Descriptors.MolWt, Crippen.MolLogP, CalcTPSA, CalcNumHBD/HBA, CalcNumRotatableBonds, CalcNumAromaticRings, CalcFractionCSP3, MurckoScaffoldSmiles (cheminformatics.py:77-99).
Lipinski Ro5 implemented to spec: counts MW>500, logP>5, HBD>5, HBA>10, passes if <=1 violation (cheminformatics.py:89-90).

GSK 4/400 (MW<=400 and logP<=4) and Pfizer 3/75 (logP>3 and TPSA<75) match the published Gleeson 2008 / Hughes 2008 definitions (cheminformatics.py:94-95); verified on aspirin (GSK ok, not tox) and decylbenzene (GSK fail, tox risk) in test_cheminformatics.py:54-62.
Ligand efficiency LE=1.37*pChEMBL/heavy_atoms (Hopkins 2004) and LLE=pChEMBL-cLogP (Leeson 2007) are correct and pinned by exact-value unit tests (test_cheminformatics.py:38-45).
ECFP4 Tanimoto (Morgan radius 2, 2048 bits) and Butina clustering at 0.35 distance cutoff are real RDKit (cheminformatics.py:122-164).
SA-scorer-absent path is honest: sa_score=None when Contrib missing (line 100), and the JSON field saScore is omitted (not null) rather than fabricated (precompute_site_data.py:111-112).

The committed *.json values are internally consistent with the live analyze() output (all 40 target files carry saScore, le, lle, scaffold, pains/brenk, gskOk, pfizerToxRisk, fractionCsp3 in the exact analyze() shape).
whatIsStubOrWrapper: SA score is entirely dependency-gated: if RDKit's Contrib SA_Score tree is not importable, _HAS_SASCORE=False and every sa_score is None (cheminformatics.py:46-56,100). The current pip rdkit wheel bundles it, but the CI workflow only runs 'pip install rdkit' (.github/workflows/cheminformatics-precompute.yml:31) — on any rdkit build without Contrib, all saScore fields would silently disappear from refreshed JSON.
The whole module is a wrapper with zero original chemistry — every signal is an RDKit call; there is no fpscores file or SMARTS shipped in this repo (correctly relying on RDKit's bundled data).

PAINS/Brenk hit LISTS are truncated to the first 5 (pains_hits[:5], brenk_hits[:5], cheminformatics.py:110-111) while the *_alerts COUNT is full — the JSON 'pains'/'brenk' arrays can under-represent the true alert set.

Per-target JSON files are only rewritten when live ChEMBL triage succeeds; on failure the target is skipped (precompute_site_data.py:88-89), leaving the previously committed file on disk unchanged (a wrapper around a flaky external data source, not a recomputation).

inaccuracies.title: 'clean' label ignores Brenk structural alerts
inaccuracies.claim: The 'clean' flag implies a compound passes structural-alert screening (module advertises 'structural-alert screening (PAINS, Brenk)').
inaccuracies.reality: clean = Ro5 and Veber and (PAINS==0) only; Brenk alerts are not part of the definition. 175 committed entries are labeled clean:true despite brenkAlerts>0 (e.g. ERBB2.json CHEMBL203644 brenkAlerts=1 clean=true; BRAF.json CHEMBL500659 brenkAlerts=3 clean=true).

inaccuracies.title: Egan rule is a rectangular approximation of an ellipsoidal absorption model
inaccuracies.claim: egan_ok / eganOk represents the Egan 2000 absorption rule.
inaccuracies.reality: Egan & Merz 2000 defines a confidence ellipse in PSA vs AlogP98 space; the code uses a simple rectangle TPSA<=131.6 and MolLogP<=5.88. The 131.6/5.88 are the egg's axis bounds, and it substitutes RDKit MolLogP for AlogP98. Compounds near the corners are misclassified vs the true ellipse.

inaccuracies.title: Veber rule omits the HBD+HBA<=12 alternative clause
inaccuracies.claim: veber_ok represents the Veber 2002 oral-bioavailability rule.
inaccuracies.reality: Veber 2002 is rotatable bonds<=10 AND (TPSA<=140 OR H-bond count (HBD+HBA)<=12). The code implements only rotb<=10 and tpsa<=140, so molecules with TPSA>140 but low H-bond count are flagged fail when Veber would pass them. This is a common simplification but not the full spec.

inaccuracies.title: Committed JSON is a non-reproducible live-ChEMBL snapshot; precompute can serve stale per-target data while index date advances
inaccuracies.claim: index.json 'generated' date represents the freshness of the served cheminformatics set; weekly Action 'maintains' the set.
inaccuracies.reality: Per-target descriptors are deterministic, but the compound SET comes from a live virtual_triage.py ChEMBL query, so a re-run on a later date can return different molecules. When a target's triage fails, its old JSON file is left untouched (continue) and the index entry is preserved by the merge-by-slug 'never shrink' guard, yet index.json's top-level 'generated' is overwritten with the new run's stamp — so index.json can advertise a fresh date over stale per-target files.

inaccuracies.title: saScore would silently vanish on refresh in a Contrib-less RDKit, changing JSON schema
inaccuracies.claim: Committed files all carry saScore (Ertl SA), presented as a stable field.
inaccuracies.reality: saScore is conditionally added only if _HAS_SASCORE (precompute_site_data.py:111). A precompute run on an RDKit build lacking the Contrib SA_Score tree would drop saScore from every entry without error, producing inconsistent schema vs the currently committed files (which all include it).

inaccuracies.title: PAINS/Brenk arrays truncated to 5 while count is full
inaccuracies.claim: JSON 'pains'/'brenk' arrays list the structural alerts matched.
inaccuracies.reality: Only the first 5 matches are stored (pains_hits[:5]); painsAlerts/brenkAlerts hold the true count, so a compound with >5 alerts shows a list shorter than its count.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Med-chemists and cheminformatics researchers could legitimately use this today as a fast first-pass triage/filter: the PAINS/Brenk, QED, SA, descriptors, Ro5/Veber/Egan, GSK/Pfizer, LE/LLE and Butina clustering are all real RDKit and would reproduce in any standard RDKit environment. It is appropriate for hypothesis-generation, exactly as the module disclaims. Where it would mislead: (1) the 'clean' flag is a partial gate (Ro5+Veber+no-PAINS) that a user could mistake for 'passes all structural alerts' — 175 served compounds are 'clean' yet carry Brenk alerts; (2) the served web JSON is a frozen 2026-06-23 ChEMBL snapshot, so anyone treating /explore numbers as current ChEMBL is wrong, and the never-shrink guards mean an individual target could be stale even while index.json shows a fresh date; (3) Egan/Veber are simplified forms. It is NOT a treatment tool and is correctly framed as research/triage. No oncologist or patient should derive anything clinical from it.
audits.readmeNotes: Claims that are accurate: 'real RDKit-based', 'structural-alert screening (PAINS, Brenk) via RDKit FilterCatalog', and the citations (Lipinski/Veber/Egan/Baell/Brenk/Bemis-Murcko/Bickerton QED/Ertl SAscore/Hopkins/Leeson/Gleeson/Hughes) all map to genuinely implemented, real calls. Needs caveats: (1) 'clean' should be documented as Ro5+Veber+no-PAINS only — it does NOT account for Brenk alerts, so 'clean' compounds can still carry Brenk fragments. (2) Egan and Veber are simplified rectangular/single-clause approximations, not the full published ellipse / (TPSA OR H-bond) forms. (3) SA score (saScore) is present only when RDKit's Contrib SA_Score is importable; describe it as optional and note it is omitted (not null) otherwise. (4) The web/public/data/cheminformatics/*.json are a dated live-ChEMBL snapshot (generated 2026-06-23), not live values; note the 'never shrink' merge guard means a target file can remain stale (and index.json's date can advance past stale per-target files) if a weekly triage fails. (5) PAINS/Brenk JSON arrays are truncated to 5 entries; the *_alerts count is authoritative.

audits.summary: Both files are honest about scope and stdlib-only, and dock.py genuinely refuses to fabricate scores (it shells out to the real AutoDock Vina binary or prints install help). But the "best experimental PDB" selection is shallow: it ranks PDB cross-references only by crystallographic method and resolution, completely ignoring sequence coverage, which domain is resolved, apo-vs-holo, and whether the structure is a mutant — so it can confidently download a low-resolution-but-correct… or a high-resolution structure that covers the wrong domain, is a point mutant, or has no ligand. The AlphaFold fallback never reads or surfaces real pLDDT confidence (it only prints a static "validate pLDDT" string), always grabs only the F1 fragment, and the gene→UniProt resolver takes the single top relevance hit, any of which can silently hand off an unsuitable or wrong-region structure. dock.py does not invent results, and run_pipeline only presents a docking box + a "next step: dock" command (not fake affinities) — but it labels docking "feasible: yes" purely on PDB count, and its blind-box fallback uses a `--autobox` flag that stock AutoDock Vina does not support.
whatIsReal: dock.py is a genuine wrapper, not a simulator: if `vina`/`obabel` are absent it prints INSTALL_HELP and returns 3 without producing any score (cad/dock.py:144-146, 59-70), and scores are only ever parsed from real Vina output files (cad/dock.py:120-129).
Receptor prep really strips HETATM/water to avoid docking against the co-crystal ligand (clean_receptor_pdb, cad/dock.py:77-88) and converts to PDBQT via Open Babel at pH 7.4 (cad/dock.py:91-100).
fetch_structure resolver constrains the target search to reviewed human entries (organism_id:9606 AND reviewed:true), reducing wrong-organism hits for the --target path (cad/fetch_structure.py:55).
AlphaFold URL is resolved live via the prediction API with a HEAD-probe fallback across model versions, avoiding hardcoded version 404s (cad/fetch_structure.py:76-95).

run_pipeline presents only a docking *box* and a ready-to-run command as the next step, not fabricated docking results (cad/run_pipeline.py:230-256), and dock.py output is hedged as 'approximate ranking aids — not measured affinity' (cad/dock.py:182).
whatIsStubOrWrapper: pLDDT confidence is never actually computed or surfaced — it exists only as a static note string in the report; nothing reads the B-factor/pLDDT column of the AlphaFold PDB (cad/fetch_structure.py:170). A low-confidence predicted model is downloaded and handed off to docking identically to a high-confidence one.
All real docking depends on optional external binaries (`vina`, `obabel`) being on PATH (cad/dock.py:55-56,144); with a default install nothing dockable runs.

Binding-site/box derivation depends on the chosen PDB being holo; binding_site.py just finds the largest non-solvent HETATM (cad/binding_site.py:largest_ligand) — if pick_best_pdb selects an apo structure there is simply no ligand and no box.

The blind-docking fallback emits `vina --autobox` (cad/dock.py:163), a flag that exists in smina/gnina but not upstream AutoDock Vina — so the no-center path is effectively non-functional with the very binary the file tells users to install.

inaccuracies.title: "Best" PDB ignores sequence coverage / which domain is resolved
inaccuracies.claim: Docstrings call the selected entry the "best experimental structure" / "best PDB" for the target (cad/fetch_structure.py:9-11, 98-99).
inaccuracies.reality: resolve_uniprot only captures id, Method and Resolution from each PDB cross-reference and discards the UniProt 'Chains' coverage range (cad/fetch_structure.py:62-72). pick_best_pdb ranks solely by (method preference, resolution) (cad/fetch_structure.py:98-105). A high-resolution structure of a single isolated domain or a short peptide fragment will outrank a full-length/relevant-domain structure, so it can pick a structure that does not even contain the druggable site.

inaccuracies.title: No apo/holo, mutant, or construct filtering — can select an unsuitable structure
inaccuracies.claim: Selected structure is suitable for downstream binding-site detection and docking (cad/fetch_structure.py:6-11).
inaccuracies.reality: Nothing distinguishes apo from holo, wild-type from mutant (e.g. EGFR T790M, kinase-dead, or engineered constructs), or chimeric fusion constructs; selection is purely method+resolution. A catalytically-dead mutant or an apo form can be chosen as 'best', and binding_site.py would then derive no box or a box around a crystallisation additive.

inaccuracies.title: AlphaFold pLDDT confidence is claimed-needed but never read
inaccuracies.claim: Report note: 'Predicted model — validate confidence (pLDDT) before docking' implies confidence is considered (cad/fetch_structure.py:170).
inaccuracies.reality: No code parses pLDDT (the per-residue B-factor column) or gates the handoff on it; a model with very low pLDDT is downloaded and reported the same as a high-confidence one, and run_pipeline proceeds to suggest docking against it.
inaccuracies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:158-171; only occurrence of 'pLDDT' is the literal string at line 170 (grep)

inaccuracies.title: AlphaFold fetch only ever gets fragment F1 — truncates large proteins
inaccuracies.claim: Falls back to 'the AlphaFold predicted model' for the target (cad/fetch_structure.py:11, 157).
inaccuracies.reality: Both the API-derived and probed URLs, and the output filename, are hardcoded to '-F1-' (cad/fetch_structure.py:88, 162). Proteins longer than the AlphaFold per-fragment limit (>2700 aa) are split into F1, F2, … fragments; only F1 is fetched, silently yielding a partial structure for large targets.

inaccuracies.title: Gene→UniProt resolution takes top relevance hit (size=1) — can fetch the wrong protein
inaccuracies.claim: resolve_uniprot 'resolves a target/gene name to a reviewed human UniProt entry' (cad/fetch_structure.py:53-54).
inaccuracies.reality: Query is `(gene:{name} OR protein_name:{name})` with size=1 and results[0] taken unconditionally (cad/fetch_structure.py:55-60). For ambiguous or short gene symbols, or names that match many protein_name fields, the top relevance hit may not be the intended gene, and there is no exact-symbol verification — so the whole structure/box/dock chain can silently run on the wrong molecule.

inaccuracies.title: Blind-box fallback uses `--autobox`, unsupported by upstream AutoDock Vina
inaccuracies.claim: Omit --center for a blind box over the whole receptor (cad/dock.py:14-16, 160-163).
inaccuracies.reality: The fallback runs `vina ... --autobox` (cad/dock.py:163). Upstream AutoDock Vina has no --autobox option (it requires center/size or a --config / Vina-GPU; --autobox is a smina/gnina extension). With the exact binary the wrapper instructs users to install, the no-center path errors out rather than performing blind docking.
inaccuracies.evidence: cad/dock.py:160-163; install instructions point to ccsb-scripps/AutoDock-Vina at cad/dock.py:46-48

inaccuracies.title: Pipeline labels docking 'feasible' from PDB count alone
inaccuracies.claim: SUMMARY/manifest: 'PDB structures: N (docking feasible: yes/no)' keyed on pdb_count (cad/run_pipeline.py:175).
inaccuracies.reality: Feasibility is decided solely by whether any PDB cross-reference exists, not by whether the structure covers the binding site, is holo, or has a definable pocket. A target with only an apo or wrong-domain PDB is reported as 'docking feasible: yes'.

inaccuracies.title: Direct --pdb download is unvalidated and trusted blindly
inaccuracies.claim: '--pdb <ID>' downloads that entry (cad/fetch_structure.py:113-119).
inaccuracies.reality: _download's return value is ignored and there is no check that the entry corresponds to the intended target; the report prints 'Fetched structure' regardless of content. CIF-format fetches also bypass clean_receptor_pdb in dock.py (only .pdb is cleaned, cad/dock.py:95), so a co-crystal ligand in a .cif receptor stays in the receptor during docking.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Useful today only for a structure-aware researcher/computational chemist who treats it as a convenience fetcher + a faithful Vina launcher and manually checks the chosen PDB. dock.py is trustworthy in the narrow sense that it will not invent affinities. The danger is automation-by-naivety: the 'best PDB' picker can hand a med-chemist the wrong domain, an apo form, or a mutant without flagging it; the AlphaFold path can pass a low-pLDDT or F1-truncated model straight into a docking command while only printing a 'validate pLDDT' string it never enforces; and the gene resolver can resolve the wrong protein for ambiguous symbols. The pipeline's 'docking feasible: yes' and ready-to-run dock command can lead a non-expert to believe a target is dock-ready when the structure is unsuitable. It would mislead anyone (oncologist, non-structural researcher) who assumes 'best structure' or 'feasible' implies validated suitability. No fabricated docking scores are presented, which is the one place it stays honest.
audits.readmeNotes: Limitations doc should state plainly: (1) "best experimental PDB" = lowest-resolution X-ray/EM cross-reference only; it does NOT consider sequence coverage, resolved domain, apo vs holo, point mutations, or engineered constructs, and can select a structure that lacks or misrepresents the binding site (cad/fetch_structure.py:98-105). (2) AlphaFold models are NOT confidence-gated — pLDDT is never read; only fragment F1 is fetched, truncating large proteins (cad/fetch_structure.py:88,162,170). (3) gene/target name resolution takes the single top UniProt hit with no exact-symbol check and can return the wrong protein (cad/fetch_structure.py:55-60). (4) dock.py does not fabricate scores, but the documented blind-box fallback `--autobox` is not a stock AutoDock Vina option and will fail (cad/dock.py:163); 'docking feasible' in SUMMARY is based only on PDB existence (cad/run_pipeline.py:175). The pipeline presents a docking box and a ready-to-run command but never presents docking results unless the real Vina binary is run.

audits.area: Documentation reconciliation: limitations & overclaims across README.md, docs/ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md, docs/REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md, docs/IMPLEMENTATION.md
audits.summary: The repo is, by its own internal docs, an honest in-silico front-end triage tool: target resolution + ligand ranking + structure fetch + docking-box geometry + RDKit liabilities + a heuristic cost-benefit model, all provenance-tagged and re-verifiable from public DBs. The deeper design docs (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md, REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md) are unusually candid and already enumerate most real limitations — wrapper-only docking, query-design un-checked, heuristic scoring, prose/news not audited, structure files not byte-verified, holo-only binding box. The tension is between confident headline framing ("auditable evidence engine," "make fabrication impossible to hide," docking marked "implemented") and the buried caveats that qualify each of those claims one or two layers down. A consolidated Limitations section should hoist the ANTI-HALLUCINATION/ROADMAP carve-outs to the top level and reconcile the docking "implemented vs wrapper-only-not-run" and stage-numbering contradictions.
whatIsReal: Stage 1 target/druggability dossier from UniProt+ChEMBL — ROADMAP table marks 'implemented' (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:17), code cad/target_report.py.
Stage 2 ligand-based virtual triage on real ChEMBL bioactivities, pChEMBL ranking + drug-likeness, novelty filter, CSV export — 'implemented' (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:18,49-64).
Stage 3 structure acquisition (best X-ray PDB or AlphaFold fallback) — 'implemented' cad/fetch_structure.py (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:19).
Stage 4 docking box from co-crystal ligand envelope, deterministic stdlib geometry — 'implemented' cad/binding_site.py (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:20).

Stage 6 cheminformatics/liabilities (PAINS/Brenk, GSK 4/400, Pfizer 3/75, SA score, Murcko scaffolds, Butina clustering, LE/LLE) — 'implemented', gated on RDKit (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:22; IMPLEMENTATION.md:95).
Stage 7 triage report orchestration to SUMMARY.md with partial-dossier degradation — 'implemented' cad/run_pipeline.py (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:23,28-35).
Provenance manifest + independent verifier with byte-equal SMILES check and deterministic recomputes; raises on origin='model' (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:13-52).
Refusal behavior: unresolved target writes nothing and exits non-zero; empty/cost-benefit-only run never earns a green banner (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:67-78).
examples/sample-run-egfr/ exists with a real committed run (dossier.json, hits.csv, liabilities.json, provenance.json, etc.) — confirmed on disk.
cad/requirements.txt exists; dock.py has a real check_deps()/--check path that probes for vina+obabel on PATH (cad/dock.py:55-66).
whatIsStubOrWrapper: Stage 5 structure-based docking (cad/dock.py): a thin wrapper on AutoDock Vina that runs only if vina+obabel binaries are present; ROADMAP labels it 'implemented' (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:21) but ANTI-HALLUCINATION admits it is 'wrapper-present but not exercised in CI, because CI does not install Vina' (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:57-58). Effectively wrapper-only/unvalidated in the default install.

Pocket detection for apo targets: fpocket/P2Rank are 'still optional' (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:20) and only listed as future how-to (lines 78-79) — not wired in. The binding box therefore only works for holo (co-crystal-ligand) structures; an AlphaFold/apo structure has no automated pocket.

Cost-benefit model (cad/cost_benefit.py): 'static benchmarks' (IMPLEMENTATION.md:99); scoring weights, oncology factor, gross margin are 'transparent, editable, non-validated design choices... not fitted to any benchmark' (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:60-65); modality/phase-level, 'not a target-specific prediction' (README.md:226, 38).
Triage scoring weights (virtual_triage.py): heuristic combination of real inputs, 'a triage ordering, never a prediction of efficacy' (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:60-65).
RDKit-dependent outputs (hits.sdf, liabilities.json, 2-D similarity, descriptors) only produced if RDKit is installed (README.md:115-116; REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:66-67).
cad/intel/ news digests (news_update.py, Tavily): 'leads to verify at the primary source — never validated facts' and require an API key (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:88-89; IMPLEMENTATION.md:102).
Generative/de-novo design and FEP are explicitly out of scope / not implemented (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:94-100).

inaccuracies.title: Docking stage labeled 'implemented' in roadmap but is wrapper-only and never run in CI
inaccuracies.claim: ROADMAP status column: stage 5 docking '**implemented** — cad/dock.py (gated on the real binaries)'
inaccuracies.reality: ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:57-58 concedes docking is 'wrapper-present but not exercised in CI, because CI does not install Vina.' It produces no validated output by default; 'implemented' overstates relative to the other genuinely-running stages.

inaccuracies.title: README calls docking 'stage 6'; every other doc calls it stage 5
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:87 '# Docking (stage 6) needs two non-pip binaries on PATH'
inaccuracies.reality: dock.py header, the ROADMAP table, and ROADMAP prose all number docking as stage 5 and the cheminformatics/liability pass as stage 6 (cad/dock.py:3 'stage 5'; REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:21 row 5 = docking, line 28 'cheminformatics liability pass (stage 6)'). README has the two swapped.

inaccuracies.title: Live-site URL/branding inconsistent across docs
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:17 advertises the live app at provenika.com; clone URL is github.com/Aroxora/provenika
inaccuracies.reality: IMPLEMENTATION.md:275 says the live site is https://cancer-cure-osint.web.app (Firebase project cancer-cure-osint), and the deploy commands target that project — a different domain than the README's provenika.com. A reader cannot tell which is canonical.

inaccuracies.title: Headline 'make fabrication impossible to hide' is broader than what verify.py actually proves
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:7 '...and make fabrication impossible to hide'; README.md:35 verifier 'Re-pulls every figure from its live source'
inaccuracies.reality: Count checks only re-run the same query logic, proving reproducibility/freshness, not query-design correctness: 'A wrong-but-stable query would pass' (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:42-44,90). Free-text prose, news digests, and structure-file byte-integrity are explicitly NOT covered (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:85-93). So a class of fabrication (wrong query design, fabricated prose) is in fact hideable.

inaccuracies.title: 'every figure can be traced back... and re-proven with one command' omits the carve-outs
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:12 'where every figure can be traced back to a public record and re-proven with one command'
inaccuracies.reality: The guarantee covers only figures in machine-readable artifacts; SUMMARY.md prose, target_report.py read-out lines, and cad/intel/ digests are unaudited summaries/leads (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:85-89). README's own 'Honest scope' paragraph (lines 41-44) qualifies this but the headline does not.

inaccuracies.title: Binding-box stage presented as general but only works for holo structures
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:118 'binding_site.json — Docking box computed from the co-crystal ligand envelope'; ROADMAP stage 4 'implemented'
inaccuracies.reality: The box derivation needs a co-crystal ligand; for apo or AlphaFold structures there is none, and fpocket/P2Rank are 'still optional'/not wired (REAL-CAD-ROADMAP.md:20). So stage 4 silently does not apply to the AlphaFold-fallback structures stage 3 may return.

inaccuracies.title: Repo name 'cancer-cure-agent' contradicts the stated scope
inaccuracies.claim: Repo/dir name cancer-cure-agent; IMPLEMENTATION.md:7 'must never... claim to discover or cure anything'
inaccuracies.reality: The product is renamed 'Provenika' and disclaims curing throughout (README.md:3-4,163-166), yet the repository name still asserts a cure. Pure framing/branding mismatch, neutralized only by disclaimers.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Useful today for med-chemists / computational-biology researchers as a fast, fully-cited triage front-end: resolve a target, pull measured-active ChEMBL ligands ranked by potency+drug-likeness, flag PAINS/Brenk/developability liabilities, fetch a structure, and get a Vina-ready docking box — every number re-verifiable against a public URL. It would mislead anyone who reads only the headline: docking yields no scores out-of-the-box (wrapper not run), the cost-benefit 'feasibility verdict' is a non-validated modality/phase benchmark not a target prediction, the binding box quietly fails on apo/AlphaFold structures, and the verifier's PASS does not certify the query design or any free-text claim. It is explicitly NOT for oncologists or any clinical use — no diagnosis, prognosis, dosing, or treatment recommendation — and the docs (and CI medical-safety audit) enforce that. Honest framing: a provenance/audit research aid for the cheap in-silico front of discovery, not a discovery or validation engine.
audits.readmeNotes: For the consolidated Limitations section, hoist these already-acknowledged-but-buried caveats to top level: (1) Docking (dock.py) is a wrapper only — never run in CI, produces no score without locally-installed vina+obabel; reconcile the ROADMAP 'implemented' label with ANTI-HALLUCINATION's 'wrapper-present but not exercised' (ROADMAP:21 vs ANTI-HALL:57-58). (2) verify.py PASS = reproducibility/freshness, NOT query-design correctness — 'a wrong-but-stable query would pass' (ANTI-HALL:42-44). (3) Coverage carve-outs: SUMMARY.md prose, target_report read-outs, cad/intel news digests, and structure-file byte-integrity are NOT audited (ANTI-HALL:85-93). (4) Cost-benefit + triage weights are non-validated heuristics; the feasibility verdict is modality/phase-level, not target-specific (ANTI-HALL:60-65, README:226). (5) Binding box is holo-only; apo/AlphaFold structures get no automated pocket (ROADMAP:20). (6) Triage ≠ validation; no selectivity/cell/safety, ChEMBL coverage uneven (ROADMAP:69-71). Specific phrasings to soften: README:7 'make fabrication impossible to hide' and README:12 'every figure can be traced back... re-proven with one command' overclaim relative to the prose/query-design carve-outs; the 'auditable evidence engine' headline (README:9) should sit adjacent to the wrapper-only-docking caveat. Fix concrete errors: README:87 mislabels docking as 'stage 6' (it is stage 5); reconcile provenika.com (README:17) vs cancer-cure-osint.web.app (IMPLEMENTATION:275).

audits.area: Real-world usefulness, traction/adoption claims, and the outreach system
audits.summary: This repo is an honestly-scoped "auditable evidence engine" for the cheap, in-silico front of oncology drug discovery (target triage to ligand shortlist to structure to docking-box to feasibility), where every machine-readable figure is either fetched-and-cited from a public DB or deterministically computed, with a one-command re-verifier. The CADD core and the committed EGFR sample run are genuinely real and re-runnable. But there is ZERO evidence of any real oncologist or researcher using it: utility is entirely prospective. The "outreach" system is not patient/clinician outreach at all — it is an investor-fundraising email agent, and it is not live: status.json reports overall:fail, send_enabled:false, sent:0; all 4 stored contacts are VCs (OSS Capital, Decibel, Amplify, Lux) sitting in "drafted" status; the public outreach log has zero entries. Crucially, the repo's own pitch/business docs are unusually candid: traction is left as explicit "[fill in]" placeholders, the business verdict is "pass as a venture investment," and the README leads with "an AI agent CANNOT cure cancer." So the project does NOT fabricate adoption — its main honesty risk is the gap between an impressive-looking provenance/pitch apparatus and the fact that nobody is using it and nothing has shipped to a real user.
whatIsReal: The committed EGFR sample run is a real, unedited pipeline snapshot from live public data (UniProt P00533, ChEMBL, RCSB PDB): examples/sample-run-egfr/SUMMARY.md:4-8 reports 354 PDB structures, 21342 potent ChEMBL activities, 49 known-mechanism drugs, plus a real 8A27.pdb structure and a binding box computed from the KY9 co-crystal ligand (SUMMARY.md:21-23).

A genuinely useful med-chem artifact ships: hits.csv (25 ranked ligand candidates with SMILES + ChEMBL links), hits.sdf (3-D, property-tagged for PyMOL/Maestro/docking), and liabilities.json with RDKit PAINS/Brenk + Pfizer 3/75 / GSK 4/400 developability flags (examples/sample-run-egfr/README.md:36-42, SUMMARY.md:12-16). This is a legitimately handoff-able shortlist for real downstream tools.

Enforced provenance is real and re-checkable: README.md:32-40 documents cad/provenance.py (origin-tags every figure) and cad/verify.py (re-pulls each figure live -> PASS/DRIFT/FAIL, byte-equal SMILES check vs ChEMBL), CI-gated.

The repo refuses to fake outputs: cad/dock.py is a thin AutoDock Vina wrapper that exits with install steps rather than inventing a docking score; run_pipeline.py refuses a feasibility verdict for an unresolved/fictional target (README.md:36-37).

The outreach agent code exists and is functional in dry-run: outreach/agent.py, emailer.py, store.py, monitor.py, with a real RAG-grounded drafting flow and 4 researched VC prospect profiles in outreach/.state/store.json.
Business/pitch self-analysis is real and rigorous: business/README.md and BUSINESS-MODEL-AND-FUNDING.md give a cited, skeptical 'pass as a venture investment' verdict with named comparables.
whatIsStubOrWrapper: The 'outreach' system is mislabeled relative to the user's question: it is an INVESTOR fundraising email agent, not oncologist/researcher outreach. All 4 contacts are VCs (outreach/.state/store.json: hello@oss.capital, founders@decibel.vc, info@amplifypartners.com, info@luxcapital.com), all category *_vc.

The outreach system is NOT live. web/public/data/outreach/status.json:3 overall:fail; :32-33 'Outreach monitor' status:fail 'unavailable'; :37 send_enabled:false; :42-47 sent:0, replied:0, only 4 drafts. control.json: sendEnabled:false, updatedBy:'test'. All 4 contacts are status:'drafted' — nothing was ever sent.
No actual emails have gone out: no .outbox/ directory, no monitor.log, no sent/contacted timestamps in the store; web/public/data/outreach/log.json has totals all 0 and entries:[].

The cost-benefit / feasibility verdict is a modality/phase-level benchmark from published priors, NOT a target-specific prediction — it returns identical numbers for any target with the same inputs (examples/sample-run-egfr/SUMMARY.md:26, README.md:38). The EGFR run's 'P(approval) 5.1%, benefit/cost 1.03' figures say nothing specific about EGFR.
Docking (the step that would make a hit actionable) only runs if a real AutoDock Vina + Open Babel binary is present locally; otherwise it just prints install steps (cad/dock.py per README.md:36).
All pitch 'traction' is placeholder: pitch/PITCH.md:47-56, ONE-PAGER.md:13-15, and pitch/YC.md:24 leave GitHub stars / runs / users / design partners / team / ask as literal '[fill in]'.

inaccuracies.title: No evidence of any real oncologist or researcher usage — utility is entirely prospective
inaccuracies.claim: YC.md:9-12 frames the product as producing hypotheses 'for oncology researchers'; README markets provenika.com as a live tool for 'human scientists'.
inaccuracies.reality: There is no user, usage log, design partner, or adoption artifact anywhere in the repo. The only 'users' represented are 4 VC fundraising prospects, none contacted. The public outreach log (web/public/data/outreach/log.json:5-11) has all-zero totals and zero entries.
