inaccuracies.claim: outreach/README.md:1,34-49 describes a running agent (RAG memory, AWS Lambda EventBridge daily, Firestore, 24/7 IMAP monitor) that 'sends within strict caps' and 'publishes a public log'.
inaccuracies.reality: The system is not operational: status.json overall:fail, outreach monitor 'unavailable', Firestore 'degraded', send_enabled:false, sent:0. It exists as code in dry-run only; nothing has ever been sent and no monitor is running (no monitor.log).

inaccuracies.title: README headline 'auditable oncology evidence engine' could imply a clinician-facing product
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:1 titles it 'auditable oncology evidence engine'; README.md:17 promotes 'Try it live' at provenika.com with 'disease -> targets -> triage -> structure -> models'.
inaccuracies.reality: The tool only addresses the in-silico research front (public-data target/ligand triage). It explicitly cannot help a clinician with a patient — and the README itself says so up front, so this is mitigated, not hidden. The risk is a reader skimming the title/landing claims without the disclaimer.
inaccuracies.evidence: README.md:1,3-7,14-15 (disclaimer: 'cannot cure cancer... Not medical advice'), README.md:165 ('replace docking/ADMET/wet-lab/a clinician' listed under what it does NOT do)

inaccuracies.title: Feasibility/cost-benefit numbers in the sample run can read as target-specific de-risking
inaccuracies.claim: examples/sample-run-egfr/SUMMARY.md:25-31 presents concrete EGFR figures: 'P(approval) from phase1: 5.1%', 'Expected remaining cost: $360M', 'benefit/cost 1.03', 'Marginal'.
inaccuracies.reality: These are generic modality/phase priors, identical for any small_molecule@phase1 target regardless of EGFR-specific evidence; they do not reflect EGFR at all. The doc does flag this (line 26), but the numeric presentation invites over-reading.
inaccuracies.evidence: examples/sample-run-egfr/SUMMARY.md:26 ('Identical inputs give identical figures for any target; it does not know this target')

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Realistically useful TODAY, to a med-chemist or computational/oncology researcher: (1) Fast, keyless target triage and a druggability dossier (PDB count, ChEMBL activity volume, known-mechanism drugs) to decide whether a target is worth pursuing — genuinely handy as a first-pass screen, though Open Targets does the prioritization more rigorously for free. (2) A cited 25-ligand shortlist (hits.csv/hits.sdf with SMILES + ChEMBL links + RDKit PAINS/Brenk/developability flags) that can be handed straight into real docking/ADMET tools — a legitimate time-saver, provided the user treats it as an unvalidated similarity-triage starting point, not a ranking of efficacy. (3) A best experimental structure + a computed docking box to bootstrap a real AutoDock Vina run. (4) The provenance/verify mechanism is a real differentiator for anyone who must defend a number to a reviewer or tumor board. Where it would MISLEAD: anyone reading the feasibility verdict (P(approval), benefit/cost) as target-specific de-risking — it is a generic phase/modality prior; anyone treating the ligand ranking as predicted potency/efficacy (it is structural similarity triage, no binding prediction without the optional Vina step); and any clinician expecting patient-level guidance — explicitly out of scope. It is NOT useful to a practicing oncologist for any patient-facing decision, and the repo correctly says so.
audits.readmeNotes: The README is unusually honest and largely avoids overclaiming traction: it leads with 'an AI agent CANNOT cure cancer' (README.md:3-7), keeps all pitch traction as explicit '[fill in]' placeholders (pitch/PITCH.md:47-56, YC.md:24, ONE-PAGER.md:13), and the business docs deliver a candid 'pass as a venture investment' verdict (business/README.md:9-10). Caveats worth adding: (1) README.md:211 describes the outreach system as one that 'sends, classifies replies, and schedules follow-ups' — true only of the code path; in reality it has never sent anything (status.json sent:0, send_enabled:false), and a reader could infer an active campaign. (2) The title 'auditable oncology evidence engine' (README.md:1) plus the provgenerika.com 'try it live' framing (README.md:17) should keep the research-only disclaimer adjacent, since the word 'oncology' invites a clinical reading. (3) Nowhere does the README claim users/oncologists/adoption — that gap is honest, but it also means any external 'who uses this' narrative is currently empty, and the investor outreach (4 unsent VC drafts) is the only 'real-world' activity, which is fundraising, not adoption.

audits.area: Node/TypeScript OSINT tool layer (src/tools/cancer/, cancer-cli, tool-count + keyless one-liners)
audits.summary: The "63 OSINT tools" number is literally accurate — createCancerTools() in src/tools/cancer/index.ts aggregates 14 modules whose ToolDefinition names total exactly 63, and cicd/generate_readme.py:31 computes that live, so the README count is not fabricated. But the "OSINT / search live" framing is an overclaim: only ~21 of the 63 actually hit free public APIs keyless (PubMed/NCBI, ClinicalTrials.gov, cBioPortal, ChEMBL, UniProt, KEGG, Open Targets). The remaining ~42 split into (a) ~13 thin Tavily query-wrappers in research/ that require TAVILY_API_KEY and throw without it, plus several discovery/biotech tools that also gate on Tavily, and (b) ~25 static curated-dataset / template-generator tools (oral-therapy lists, gap-analysis catalogs, CAR-T/mRNA/IND "design" scaffolds, hardcoded treatment/biomarker heuristics) that query no external source at all. The 6 keyless one-liners are genuine: osintMode.ts dispatches each to a live public-API client with no key. The "8 data sources" self-test is accurate — runConnectivityTest probes exactly 8 endpoints live.
whatIsReal: The 6 keyless one-liners genuinely dispatch to live public APIs with no key. src/headless/osintMode.ts:19-26 maps each pattern to a real handler: PubMedSearch (NCBI eutils), ClinicalTrialSearch (clinicaltrials.gov v2), GetProteinInfo (UniProt), FindPathwaysForGene (KEGG), FindDrugsForTarget (ChEMBL), FindTargetsForDisease (Open Targets); runOsintMode (lines 66-86) calls the tool handler directly, no LLM/key.

The underlying clients hit real free endpoints with no API key required: pubmedClient.ts:116 (eutils.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov, key only raises rate limit), clinicalTrialsClient.ts:151 (clinicaltrials.gov/api/v2), uniprotClient.ts:89 (rest.uniprot.org), keggClient.ts:48 (rest.kegg.jp), chemblClient.ts:110 (ebi.ac.uk/chembl), openTargetsClient.ts:39 (api.platform.opentargets.org).

PubMedSearch handler uses an optional NCBI key purely for rate-limit and works keyless: src/tools/cancer/literature/pubmedTools.ts handler reads getSecretValue('NCBI_API_KEY') ?? undefined and pubmedClient.ts:117 sets 3/sec without key vs 10/sec with.

The tool count is computed live, not hardcoded: cicd/generate_readme.py:30-31 require()s dist/tools/cancer/index.js and prints createCancerTools().length; the 14 modules in src/tools/cancer/index.ts:42-57 do sum to 63 distinct tool names.

The genuinely-live keyless OSINT layer (~21 tools): 3 PubMed, 3 ClinicalTrials, 4 cBioPortal genomics (variantTools.ts), 5 drug/target (ChEMBL+UniProt, targetTools.ts), 1 Open Targets (diseaseTargetTools.ts), 5 KEGG pathway (pathwayTools.ts).

The --self-test really probes 8 live data sources: src/bin/cancer-cli.ts:204-268 lists PubMed/NCBI, ClinicalTrials.gov, UniProt, RCSB PDB, cBioPortal, KEGG, ChEMBL, Open Targets and calls client.testConnection() on each — matching README's '8 data sources' claim.
whatIsStubOrWrapper: 13 research/ tools are thin Tavily query-variant wrappers that all funnel into the same TavilyClient.search and REQUIRE a key: createTavilyClient (src/datasources/research/tavilyClient.ts:506-511) throws 'Tavily API key required' when TAVILY_API_KEY is unset. Tools: SearchDrugResearch, SearchClinicalTrials, SearchBiomarkerResearch, VerifyMedicalClaim, SearchFDAApprovals, SearchNCCNGuidelines, SearchResistanceMechanisms, SearchTherapeuticTargets, SearchLiterature, SearchPrecisionMedicine, SearchImmunotherapy, SearchDrugInteractions, GetLatestResearchNews (researchTools.ts:302-571). They differ only by query prefix.

These research wrappers functionally duplicate the live-API tools: research/SearchClinicalTrials (Tavily) vs clinical/ClinicalTrialSearch (real clinicaltrials.gov); research/SearchLiterature vs literature/PubMedSearch; research/SearchTherapeuticTargets vs drug-discovery target tools.

discovery/oralTherapyTools.ts tools (FindOralTherapy, GetOralTherapyByTarget, ListAllOralTherapies) serve a hardcoded list of ~33 FDA drugs defined inline (oralTherapyTools.ts:39-425); CompileTargetedTherapyBrief additionally calls Tavily (getClient at line 537, which THROWS if no key, oralTherapyTools.ts:27-29).

discovery/oralDrugPipeline.ts (ListAllOralDrugCandidates, FindOralPillForCancer, GenerateOralDrugSummary) queries no external source — pure static curated dataset (no fetch/client references in file).

discovery/cadTools.ts DesignCARTTherapy, DesignmRNAVaccine, GenerateFDAProposal are template/scaffold generators (honestly described as 'educational scaffold, not a validated construct'); IdentifyTherapeuticTargets/SearchEmergingSolutions call Tavily via getClient (cadTools.ts:89,309).

discovery/gapAnalysisTools.ts list tools (GetUnmetNeedStats, ListNoScreeningCancers, ListRareCancers, ListEmergingTech, AnalyzeAllGaps) return a hardcoded gap catalog (gapAnalysisTools.ts:124-288); ProposeCADSolution and SearchEmergingSolutions call Tavily (getClient at 368,485) which throws without a key.

patient/treatmentTools.ts (RecommendTreatment, StratifyRisk, AssessTreatmentResponse) are hardcoded heuristic knowledge bases with no external source; the risk model is explicitly labeled 'Composite Risk Score (non-validated heuristic)' (treatmentTools.ts:463).

patient/biomarkerTools.ts (AnalyzeBiomarker, InterpretBiomarkerPanel, GetPrognosticAssessment) are hardcoded; InterpretBiomarkerPanel carries a stub comment 'Actual Biomarker objects would be fetched from database in production' (biomarkerTools.ts:220) and returns 'Reference range not available in database' (line 132).
biotech/curativeTools.ts SearchCurativeBiotech uses Tavily (getClient at curativeTools.ts:221, requires key); GetCARTDetails/AssessCurativePotential/DesignCurativeProtocol are canned/templated.

inaccuracies.title: README frames all 63 as live-searching OSINT tools, but only ~21 query live public APIs keyless
inaccuracies.claim: README.md:159-160 says the framework can 'search PubMed / ClinicalTrials.gov / cBioPortal / ChEMBL / KEGG / RCSB PDB / UniProt live (63 OSINT tools)', implying the 63 are live OSINT lookups.
inaccuracies.reality: Of the 63, only ~21 hit those live public APIs. ~13 research/ tools require a paid TAVILY_API_KEY (createTavilyClient throws without it), and ~25 are static curated-dataset or template-generator tools that contact no external source (oralDrugPipeline.ts, gapAnalysis list tools, cadTools design scaffolds, treatmentTools, biomarkerTools).

inaccuracies.title: Tool count is real but inflated by thin Tavily query-variants and static list/template tools
inaccuracies.claim: '63 OSINT tools' (README.md:160,234) presented as the framework's OSINT capability surface.
inaccuracies.reality: createCancerTools().length is genuinely 63 (index.ts:42-57), so the number is not fabricated — but it is padded: 13 research tools differ only by a hardcoded query prefix into the same Tavily.search, and several duplicate the live-API tools (SearchClinicalTrials vs ClinicalTrialSearch; SearchLiterature vs PubMedSearch).

inaccuracies.title: Tavily-gated tools are presented alongside keyless OSINT without a key caveat
inaccuracies.claim: README's OSINT section (README.md:170-182) emphasizes 'keyless' and 'no API key', and many tool descriptions (e.g. CompileTargetedTherapyBrief, ProposeCADSolution) read as available capabilities.
inaccuracies.reality: CompileTargetedTherapyBrief, SearchCurativeBiotech, ProposeCADSolution, SearchEmergingSolutions, IdentifyTherapeuticTargets and all 13 research/ tools throw 'Tavily API key required' at runtime when TAVILY_API_KEY is unset — they are neither keyless nor free.

inaccuracies.title: InterpretBiomarkerPanel contains an unfinished 'in production' stub path
inaccuracies.claim: AnalyzeBiomarker/InterpretBiomarkerPanel presented as a working biomarker interpretation capability.
inaccuracies.reality: The handler returns a panel object with a TODO-style comment that real Biomarker objects 'would be fetched from database in production', and falls back to 'Reference range not available in database' — i.e. it is a partial hardcoded heuristic, not a real biomarker database lookup.

audits.usefulnessAssessment: Useful today for researchers/med-chemists doing keyless literature and target triage: the 6 one-liners and the ~21 live-API tools (PubMed, ClinicalTrials.gov, cBioPortal, ChEMBL, UniProt, KEGG, Open Targets) genuinely return cited, re-verifiable public-database results without any key, and --self-test honestly confirms 8 live endpoints. It would mislead a user who reads '63 OSINT tools' as 63 live external lookups: ~13 research tools silently need a paid Tavily key, and ~25 'discovery/patient' tools (oral-drug pipeline, gap analysis, CAR-T/mRNA/IND 'design', treatment/biomarker recommendations) are static curated datasets or template scaffolds — they look authoritative but query nothing live and, for the treatment/biomarker/prognosis tools, are explicitly non-validated heuristics that no clinician should act on. The tool descriptions themselves are mostly honest ('reference data only', 'educational scaffold', 'non-validated heuristic'), so the gap is in the README's aggregate framing, not the per-tool docstrings.
audits.readmeNotes: README.md:159-160 ('search ... live (63 OSINT tools)') should distinguish the ~21 live-keyless public-API tools from the ~13 Tavily-key-gated research wrappers and the ~25 static curated/template tools; as written it implies all 63 are live OSINT lookups. README.md:178-179 ('These six research one-liners ... need no API key') is accurate and well-scoped — but the broader '63 OSINT tools' headline (also README.md:234) should add a one-line caveat that the research/discovery tools require TAVILY_API_KEY or return curated reference data rather than live queries. The '8 data sources' / '--self-test live-check all 8' claim (README.md:173,180) is accurate (cancer-cli.ts:204-268). Worth noting the one-liners cover only 6 of those 8 sources (no one-liner for cBioPortal or RCSB PDB).

verifies.title: Preclinical approval prior attributed to BIO but not in BIO report
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:35-37 (LOA_FROM_PHASE header "overall, BIO 2011-2020" plus preclinical:0.060 with "rough: ~6%" comment); cad/cost_benefit.py:121 (sources: "BIO/Informa CDSR 2011-2020; ...")
verifies.correctedStatement: The preclinical likelihood-of-approval value (0.060) is a rough author estimate, not a published figure: the BIO/Informa/QLS Clinical Development Success Rates 2011-2020 report cited as the source only reports clinical-phase rates (Phase I onward), so the preclinical-to-approval number is surfaced under a citation that does not actually contain it.
verifies.note: The four clinical-phase values (phase1 0.079, phase2 0.151, phase3 0.460, filed 0.906) do match the real BIO 2011-2020 report, so the citation is accurate for those. Only the preclinical row is unsupported by the cited source. The code does hedge it inline ("rough: ~6%"), but the per-phase header comment (line 35) and the assumptions "sources" string (line 121) both attribute the whole dict — including preclinical — to BIO/Informa CDSR, so the misattribution is real albeit narrow.

verifies.title: Risk-adjusted gross profit mislabeled as 'revenue'
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:86-87 (gross_margin=0.80; risk_adj_rev = loa * peak_annual_rev * years_at_peak * gross_margin), 109 (key 'risk_adjusted_revenue_musd'), 139 (printed 'Risk-adjusted revenue')
verifies.correctedStatement: The cost-benefit tool's `risk_adjusted_revenue_musd` output and its "Risk-adjusted revenue" printout are actually risk-adjusted gross profit, since the value is multiplied by an 0.80 gross-margin factor (cad/cost_benefit.py:87), so the benefit-cost ratio compares profit to cost despite the "revenue" label.
verifies.note: Verified directly in cad/cost_benefit.py. The peak_annual_rev (line 85, incidence*price*penetration) is genuine revenue, but risk_adj_rev applies loa * years_at_peak * 0.80 gross_margin, making the labeled "revenue" field a gross-profit quantity. The mislabel is real, not overstated; gross_margin is also listed in assumptions (line 120) so the calculation is transparent, but the field/print names remain inaccurate. No external spec is involved.

verifies.title: No time-value discounting over a ~9.5-year horizon inflates the benefit/cost ratio
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:87 (risk_adj_rev = loa * peak_annual_rev * years_at_peak * gross_margin — no discount term); cad/cost_benefit.py:45-51 (phase durations sum to 9.5 yrs before launch); cad/cost_benefit.py:90 (bcr = risk_adj_rev / cost_dollars); no discount-rate flag in argparse at lines 152-165
verifies.correctedStatement: The cost-benefit model values future peak-sales gross profit at face value with no NPV/discount-rate parameter, even though sales begin only after summed phase durations of ~9.5 years from preclinical, so its benefit/cost ratio overstates a properly time-discounted rNPV.
verifies.note: Independently confirmed by reading the whole file. Line 87 applies loa, years_at_peak, and an 0.80 gross margin but no discount factor; line 90 divides this by undiscounted remaining cost. There is no discount/NPV flag in main()'s argparse (lines 152-165). Minor refinement to the audit's wording: gross_margin is applied so the figure is gross profit (not raw revenue), and the cost side is also undiscounted — but because revenue is realized years after spend, omitting discounting still inflates BCR, so the claim stands.

verifies.title: Asymmetric risk treatment: revenue is probability-weighted, cost is not
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:81 (exp_cost = sum of all remaining phase costs * mult, no LOA weighting, no discount) vs cad/cost_benefit.py:87 (risk_adj_rev = loa * peak_annual_rev * years_at_peak * gross_margin); result key labeled "expected_remaining_cost_musd" at line 106 and docstring "expected remaining development cost" at line 8.
verifies.correctedStatement: In the cost-benefit triage, revenue is risk-adjusted by multiplying by the likelihood of approval while the "expected remaining cost" is the full deterministic sum of all remaining per-phase costs (scaled only by a modality multiplier), with no probability-of-reaching-phase weighting and no time discounting on either side; so benefit and cost are risk-adjusted on inconsistent bases and the benefit/cost ratio is not a true rNPV/expected-value (it tends to be conservative, since probability-weighting later costs would lower expected cost).
verifies.note: Verified directly in cad/cost_benefit.py. The audit's technical observation is accurate: line 87 weights revenue by LOA, lines 80-81 sum the full remaining cost path unweighted and undiscounted. Worth a small correction to the audit's framing: this asymmetry biases the BCR downward (conservative), not upward, because a proper rNPV would weight later-phase costs by the (sub-1) probability of reaching them, reducing expected cost and raising the ratio. The file's own docstring already disclaims it as a "rough go/no-go triage aid... NOT financial advice, a valuation" (lines 13-14), but the "expected remaining cost" label (lines 8, 106) does overstate what is actually a full conditional forward-path cost.

verifies.title: Wong et al. 2019 cited for per-phase cost it does not provide
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:19-20 (Wong+DiMasi jointly cited for "per-phase out-of-pocket cost & duration"); :38 (phase1 LOA=0.079, labeled BIO 2011-2020, not Wong's ~13.8%); :47-49 (costs 25/60/255 $M, DiMasi-lineage); :121 (sources string lists "Wong et al. 2019")
verifies.correctedStatement: The cost-benefit model cites Wong, Siah & Lo (2019) alongside DiMasi et al. (2016) as the source for per-phase out-of-pocket costs, but Wong et al. estimated trial success rates and durations rather than development costs; the encoded cost figures (e.g., 25/60/255 $M) are of DiMasi lineage and the success rates actually used (e.g., 7.9% from Phase 1) are attributed to BIO/Informa 2011-2020, so none of Wong's specific numbers are the ones encoded.
verifies.note: Confirmed but with one nuance: the docstring bundles "cost & duration" together, and Wong is a legitimate source for trial durations/success rates in general — so it is not a wholly bogus citation, only mis-scoped (it does not provide cost data, and its own headline PoS of ~13.8% is overridden by BIO's 7.9% at line 38). The encoded durations (1.5/2.5/3.0 yr) are round planning heuristics, not Wong's exact values either. This is a citation/attribution accuracy issue rather than a runtime spec mismatch; the cleanest fix is to attribute per-phase costs to DiMasi 2016 only and reserve Wong 2019 for success-rate/duration context.

verifies.title: Verdict BCR thresholds have no cited or derived basis
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:92-99 (thresholds 5/1.5/0.8 hardcoded, no comment/source); contrast cad/cost_benefit.py:35-42, 44-51, 53-68 which cite or label every other constant; bcr computed undiscounted at cad/cost_benefit.py:90
verifies.correctedStatement: The go/no-go verdict bands (BCR >= 5, >= 1.5, >= 0.8) in cad/cost_benefit.py are hardcoded round-number cutoffs with no cited source, derivation, or sensitivity analysis — unlike the LOA, cost, and multiplier constants in the same file, which are sourced or explicitly labeled heuristic — and they convert an undiscounted, gross-margin-revenue-over-cost ratio into the tool's single headline label.
verifies.note: Confirmed against the actual file. The thresholds at lines 92-99 carry no citation while the file otherwise documents every constant, and the BCR (line 90) applies no discounting. Slightly mitigated by the module's heavy 'rough triage aid / order-of-magnitude' disclaimers (lines 12-21, 146-147), but the specific numeric cutoffs that drive the headline verdict are genuinely unvalidated, so the audit stands.

verifies.title: Verdict reads as a target/program-specific prediction but is a pure modality x phase benchmark
verifies.evidence: cad/cost_benefit.py:76 (loa = LOA_FROM_PHASE[phase] * oncology factor); :105 (probability_of_approval = round(loa,4)); :90-99 (verdict derived from bcr, which uses user market inputs at :85,:87,:81); :12-14 (docstring already labels all values as published benchmarks / triage aid, not a clinical claim)
verifies.correctedStatement: The cost_benefit tool's probability_of_approval is a fixed industry benchmark (LOA_FROM_PHASE[phase] optionally times a single oncology factor) with no target-, molecule-, or biology-specific input, so any program at the same phase and oncology flag gets the identical approval probability; the headline verdict additionally reflects user-entered market assumptions (incidence, price, penetration, modality cost), but never any program-specific scientific evidence.
verifies.note: Core technical claim is confirmed: at line 76 the approval probability is purely a phase lookup x oncology flag, with zero biology/target/molecule input. However the audit overstates two things: (1) it says every same-phase/modality/oncology program gets 'the same verdict' — the verdict (lines 92-99) is a function of bcr, which varies with user-entered incidence/price/penetration/years and the modality cost multiplier, so verdicts can differ; only the approval probability is truly identical. (2) It frames the code as masquerading as a per-proposal/target-specific prediction, but the docstring (lines 12-14, 16-21) and CLI output already explicitly and repeatedly disclose that every value is a published, editable benchmark and 'a rough go/no-go triage aid — NOT financial advice, a valuation, or a clinical claim,' and print each assumption/source alongside the result. So the limitation is real but already substantially disclosed; verdict is 'partly'.

verifies.title: Drug-likeness score double-counts Lipinski space (QED + Ro5)
verifies.evidence: cad/virtual_triage.py:239 (return round(0.6 * qed_part + 0.4 * ro5_part, 3)); docstring line 234 "QED + Lipinski Ro5 compliance"; ro5_part penalty line 238 (max(0.0, 1.0 - 0.34 * ro5)); pChEMBL cap line 244 (min(pchembl, 11.0) / 11.0); non-validated disclaimer lines 228-232
verifies.correctedStatement: The drug-likeness score linearly combines QED (0.6) with a Ro5-violation term (0.4), so Lipinski properties (MW, logP, H-bond donors/acceptors) that QED already incorporates are partly counted a second time; the 0.34-per-violation penalty and the pChEMBL/11 potency cap are hardcoded design constants, not fitted parameters, and the code explicitly labels the whole score a transparent, non-validated triage heuristic rather than an efficacy prediction.
verifies.note: Confirmed: the math in the file matches the audit and the property overlap between QED and the separate Ro5 term is real. There is no spec being violated (SPEC MISMATCH false is correct) — it is a deliberate heuristic. Severity is partly mitigated by lines 228-232, which already disclose the weights/penalties/cap are non-validated design choices, so this is a known-and-flagged limitation rather than a hidden bug. A README note should keep that mitigation in view and avoid implying the score claims predictive validity.

verifies.title: Box center (mean of atoms) and box size (min/max extent) are computed on inconsistent bases
verifies.evidence: cad/binding_site.py:81 (center = mean of coords) and cad/binding_site.py:82 (size = (max-min)+2*pad)
verifies.correctedStatement: In cad/binding_site.py the docking-box center is the arithmetic mean of the ligand atom coordinates (line 81) while the size is derived from the (max-min) extent plus 2x padding (line 82), so the box is centered on the atom centroid rather than the bounding-box midpoint (max+min)/2 and is therefore not symmetric about the ligand's true extent; the 8 Angstrom pad normally absorbs this offset, but for a skewed atom distribution on an axis spanning more than 16 Angstrom the centroid can sit far enough from the midpoint that the box clips part of the ligand.
verifies.note: Factual reading in the audit is accurate (center=mean at line 81, size=extent+2*pad at line 82; Vina treats the box as center +/- size/2). The reference-point inconsistency is genuine. The clipping consequence is correctly hedged: it is only possible when |mean - midpoint| > pad(8A), which mathematically requires the axis span (max-min) > 16A AND a strongly one-sided atom distribution. In practice the centroid of a real ligand is close to its midpoint and the 8A pad almost always compensates, so this is a latent design inconsistency / edge-case risk rather than a routine failure. The cited line range in the claim (8-9) refers to the docstring describing the tool's purpose; the actual computation is lines 79-83.

verifies.title: 8 Angstrom per-side padding (16 Angstrom per axis) is a hardcoded constant, large for focused re-docking
verifies.evidence: cad/binding_site.py:79 (pad: float = 8.0 default) and cad/binding_site.py:82 (size = (max-min) + 2*pad, i.e. +16 Å/axis); fpocket/P2Rank are referenced not here but in cad/dock.py:15,162 and cad/run_pipeline.py:256
verifies.correctedStatement: The docking box is the full co-crystal ligand min-max extent plus a fixed 8 Å padding per side (a hardcoded default, 16 Å added to every axis, so a ~10 Å ligand yields a ~26 Å box), derived solely from ligand extent rather than detected pocket geometry; this is larger than the tighter boxes typically recommended for redocking and should be narrowed or verified for focused docking runs.
verifies.note: Confirmed on the math: pad=8.0 default (line 79) and 2*pad=16 Å added per axis (line 82). The README/CLI string at line 122 ("ligand envelope + 8 Å padding") is itself accurate (8 Å per side), so there is no code-vs-doc spec mismatch — the audit's point is a defensible design critique that 8 Å/side is oversized for redocking, which is consistent with standard Vina guidance favoring tighter boxes. The fpocket/P2Rank references cited by the audit are in dock.py/run_pipeline.py, not in binding_site.py's docstring, but the audit's substantive claim (pad is a constant, not pocket-derived) holds.

verifies.title: Docking box ligand is the largest hetero group, not necessarily the co-crystal inhibitor
verifies.evidence: cad/binding_site.py:74 (max-atoms selection); cad/binding_site.py:31-35 (IGNORE set omits glycans/cofactors/lipids); cad/run_pipeline.py:156-157 and README.md:118 (described as "co-crystal ligand envelope")
verifies.correctedStatement: The docking box is centered on whichever single non-solvent hetero residue has the most atoms (cad/binding_site.py:74); since the IGNORE blocklist (lines 31-35) excludes only water, ions, and common cryo-additives — not cofactors (e.g. HEME/FAD/NAD), glycans, lipids, or detergents — on a cofactor- or additive-bound structure the box can land on a non-inhibitor group, and the tool never verifies that the chosen ligand occupies the pocket where the triaged hits would bind.
verifies.note: Confirmed. The "largest hetero group" claim and the missing pocket-correspondence check are both real. One refinement: residues are grouped per (res, chain, seq) (line 69), so a multi-residue glycan tree is split into separate small groups rather than counted as one — the audit's "sugar tree" example is therefore unlikely to win, but a single large cofactor residue (HEME ~43 atoms, FAD ~53, NAD ~44) plausibly can outrank a small inhibitor, so the limitation stands. Note also the code already prints an inline warning at binding_site.py:124, but the README.md:118 and provenance strings (run_pipeline.py:156-157) state it as fact without the caveat.

verifies.title: 'Potent activities' count applies no potency threshold
verifies.evidence: cad/target_report.py:61 (query filter `pchembl_value__isnull: "false"` only, no pChEMBL floor); cad/target_report.py:62 (reads page_meta.total_count as record count); cad/target_report.py:11-12 (docstring "potent measured ligands"); cad/run_pipeline.py:143 ("chembl_potent_activity_records"), :179 ("Potent ChEMBL activities"); README.md:54
verifies.correctedStatement: The "ChEMBL activities" figure is a count of ChEMBL activity records with any non-null pChEMBL value (no minimum-potency cutoff, so weak measurements down to ~pChEMBL 2 / 10 mM are included), and it counts records rather than distinct ligands, so the label "potent" and the phrase "potent measured ligands" overstate what is measured.
verifies.note: Verified directly in the source, not from the audit text. Files are under cad/, not repo root. Line 61 applies only pchembl_value__isnull=false with no floor; line 62 returns page_meta.total_count (records, not distinct molecules). The "potent"/"ligands" wording propagates to the provenance key, SUMMARY label, and README. A defensible fix is to rename to "ChEMBL bioactivity records (pChEMBL present)" or apply an explicit pchembl_value__gte threshold if "potent" is intended.

verifies.title: Ligand triage scans an arbitrary unordered slice of ChEMBL, then calls it 'the most potent'
verifies.evidence: cad/virtual_triage.py:123-131 (activity query has no order_by), :117 & :382 (while fetched < scan, --scan default 4000), :116-122 (40s budget_s breaks early with 'partial' note), :286-288 (sort by pchembl applied only to the scanned 'actives'); run_pipeline.py:151 ('ranked by a transparent potency+drug-likeness score')
verifies.correctedStatement: Ligand triage fetches ChEMBL activities with no order_by and scans only the first --scan records (default 4000, and fewer if a 40s wall-clock budget triggers), so for targets with more than ~4000 activities (e.g. EGFR) the potency+drug-likeness ranking selects the best of an arbitrary, API-order-dependent early slice rather than the globally most potent ligands, and can vary between runs when the time budget truncates the scan.
verifies.note: Verified by opening the actual file at cad/virtual_triage.py (audit cited a bare path; the file lives under cad/). The no-order_by, bounded-scan, and budget-truncation behaviors are all real and the README/docstring ('Pulls the experimentally measured potent ligands', 'Take the most potent molecules', 'ranked by a transparent potency+drug-likeness score') overstate this as a global ranking. One caveat: the audit's specific claim that ChEMBL's default order is 'activity_id ascending' is an external-API behavior not provable from the code, but it does not affect the core limitation since the scan is unordered with respect to potency either way.

verifies.title: Potency conflates assay types/relations and keeps the single most favorable value per molecule
verifies.evidence: cad/virtual_triage.py:45 (POTENCY_TYPES = {"IC50","Ki","Kd","EC50"}); :139 (stype not in POTENCY_TYPES pools all four); :147-149 (keeps max pChEMBL per molecule: if cur is None or pv > cur["pchembl"]); :125-130 & :135-149 (activity query and loop apply no standard_relation/assay_type/data_validity_comment/confidence filter); :311 (best_pchembl is one measurement, not an aggregate)
verifies.correctedStatement: Virtual triage pools IC50, Ki, Kd, and EC50 measurements into a single pChEMBL "potency" axis and, per molecule, retains only the maximum (most potent) value (cad/virtual_triage.py:45,139,147-149), without filtering on assay type/format, data-validity flags, or target/assay confidence, so the reported best_pchembl reflects the single most favorable assay rather than an aggregate or quality-screened potency.
verifies.note: Real and confirmed. One sub-claim is weaker: the code does not filter standard_relation, but ChEMBL typically only assigns pchembl_value to '=' relations, so the "censored '>' values pass" point is likely moot in practice and not verifiable from code alone — I excluded it from the corrected statement to avoid overstating. The core issues (assay-type pooling, per-molecule max cherry-pick, no assay_type/data_validity_comment/confidence filtering) are all directly evidenced in the file.

verifies.title: 'docking feasible: yes' inferred from mere existence of any PDB
verifies.evidence: cad/target_report.py:93 (pdbs = all UniProt PDB xrefs, unfiltered), :99 (pdb_count=len(pdbs)), :174-176 (feasible="yes" if pdb_count); cad/run_pipeline.py:175 ('yes' if pdb_count else 'no')
verifies.correctedStatement: The dossier reports "docking feasible: yes" whenever a target has any deposited PDB structure (pdb_count > 0), counting all UniProt PDB cross-references indiscriminately — including apo, NMR, fragment, mutant, and partial-domain or complex-only structures — so it indicates only that some experimental structure exists, not that a dockable, ligandable pocket structure is actually available.
verifies.note: Confirmed in both cited locations. pdbs at target_report.py:93 is built by filtering UniProt uniProtKBCrossReferences to database=="PDB" with no further qualification (no method, resolution, ligand, or domain-coverage check); pdb_count is its length. The "yes"/"no" feasibility flag at lines 174-176 and the SUMMARY.md line in run_pipeline.py:175 are driven purely by pdb_count > 0. No spec mismatch involved — it is a label that overstates what the underlying count establishes.

verifies.title: Best-PDB selection optimizes resolution, ignoring whether a ligand is present (holo vs apo)
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:98-105 (pick_best_pdb ranks only by X-ray/EM preference + lowest resolution, no ligand check); cad/binding_site.py:99-103 (returns exit 1 with "no non-solvent ligand found (apo structure?)" when no hetero ligand); cad/run_pipeline.py:116-122 (box set to None and silently skipped when binding_site fails; pdb_id comes from the same resolution-based pick, no holo fallback)
verifies.correctedStatement: Structure selection picks the single lowest-resolution X-ray/EM PDB for a target without checking for a bound ligand, so if that entry is apo the pipeline silently skips docking-box generation rather than searching for a co-crystallized (holo) structure of the same target.
verifies.note: Fully confirmed against the actual files. pick_best_pdb (fetch_structure.py:98-105) sorts solely by (method-preference, resolution) and never inspects HETATM/ligand content. The pipeline (run_pipeline.py:118) feeds that single best-resolution PDB ID into binding_site.py, which (lines 99-103) returns exit 1 when largest_ligand finds no non-solvent hetero group; _run_json then yields None, leaving box=None so no binding_site.json is written and no warning beyond the captured stderr. There is no code path that enumerates other PDBs to find a holo structure. The audit's description is accurate; only minor framing note: binding_site does print an apo diagnostic to stderr, but at the pipeline level it is effectively silent since _run_json discards stderr except on the generic failure print.

verifies.title: Composite/drug-likeness score constants look like meaningful quantities
verifies.correctedStatement: The 'score' column is a transparent, non-validated ranking heuristic that combines real ChEMBL inputs (pChEMBL, QED, Ro5 violations) using hardcoded normalization (pChEMBL capped and divided by 11) and arbitrary weights (0.6/0.4 or 0.45/0.3/0.25, with a 0.34-per-Ro5-violation penalty) chosen by design rather than fitted or benchmarked, so it orders candidates for triage but does not predict potency, efficacy, or developability.
verifies.note: Audit is accurate. The cited constants exist exactly as described; the only discrepancy is the file path, which is cad/virtual_triage.py (not repo-root virtual_triage.py), with line numbers matching. The limitation is real but is already explicitly and honestly disclaimed in code (lines 228-232: "a TRANSPARENT, NON-VALIDATED ranking HEURISTIC ... never a prediction of efficacy") and in the function docstrings. No spec mismatch. The remaining risk is purely presentational: a numeric 'score' in CSV/table output can still be misread as predictive by someone who skips the code comments.

verifies.title: Target resolution differs between stages; the three lookups can describe different proteins
verifies.evidence: cad/target_report.py:52-56, 79-81; cad/virtual_triage.py:81-101; cad/fetch_structure.py:55-56; cad/run_pipeline.py:137-157
verifies.correctedStatement: The pipeline resolves the target name independently at each stage — target_report's ChEMBL lookup has no exact gene-symbol guard (cad/target_report.py:52-56) while virtual_triage's does (cad/virtual_triage.py:91-98), and the two UniProt lookups (cad/target_report.py:79-81, cad/fetch_structure.py:55-56) just take the first gene-or-protein-name match — so the dossier's activity counts, the triaged ligands, the reported UniProt accession/PDB count, and the docking-box PDB can belong to different ChEMBL/UniProt proteins for the same input (e.g. "AKT1"), yet the provenance manifest reports them as one target with no cross-check that the accessions agree.
verifies.note: Verified by opening all four files (located under cad/, not repo root). The claim is fully supported and not overstated: virtual_triage's own docstring (lines 70-74) documents that the no-guard ChEMBL resolution picks substring/interactor targets (AKT1S1 for AKT1, AURKAIP1 for AURKA), and that no-guard path is exactly what target_report still uses. UniProt lookups (gene OR protein_name, size:1, results[0]) are never cross-checked against the ChEMBL target's accession, so pdb_count/activity-count/docking-box PDB can refer to distinct proteins while the manifest (run_pipeline.py:132-165) presents them as one target keyed only on the raw input string.

verifies.title: Self-consistent fabrication of potency passes the score check
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:189-205 (recompute reads best_pchembl/qed/ro5/similarity AND saved score all from the same row r); cad/virtual_triage.py:242-248 (composite_score formula); cad/verify.py:155-180 (only SMILES, not pchembl, is independently re-pulled from ChEMBL); README.md:7
verifies.correctedStatement: The score recompute only proves the `score` column is internally consistent with the other values in its own row; it re-pulls SMILES from ChEMBL for byte-equality but does not independently re-verify `best_pchembl`/`qed`/`ro5`, so a consistently edited or fully fabricated row (potency plus matching score) reproduces and passes — only an isolated edit of the score column is caught.
verifies.note: Audit is accurate. The verify.py:182-184 comment ("any drift means the score column was edited") is literally true for isolated score edits but the README:7 phrasing "make fabrication impossible to hide" overstates it. The SMILES identity check (verify.py:155-180) does independently catch edited/fabricated SMILES against ChEMBL's canonical record, so fabrication is not entirely undetectable — but numeric potency/drug-likeness inputs are not re-fetched, leaving the gap the audit describes.

verifies.title: DRIFT silently blesses changed numbers and is reported as "No fabrications"
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:83-85 (tol = max(2, abs(live)*0.10); abs(saved-live)<=tol -> DRIFT), cad/verify.py:413 ("✅ No fabrications. {n_drift} figure(s) DRIFTed"), cad/verify.py:418 (return 1 if n_fail else 0 — DRIFT exits 0), README.md:7 ("make fabrication impossible to hide")
verifies.correctedStatement: verify.py treats any saved-vs-live figure difference within max(2 absolute, 10%) — in either direction, since the comparison is absolute-valued — as a non-failing DRIFT that exits 0 and prints "No fabrications," so a figure altered within that tolerance band is not flagged as fabricated.
verifies.note: Confirmed by reading cad/verify.py (the cited verify.py is at cad/verify.py; line numbers match within a few lines). The tolerance band is symmetric (uses abs(saved-live)), so a count going DOWN is treated the same as one growing, contradicting the "growing DB" justification in the docstring; DRIFT does not affect the exit code or the success banner. The README's stronger claim that fabrication is "impossible to hide" is overstated relative to this tolerance, but the audit's technical description is accurate.

verifies.title: "Re-pulls every figure from its live source" — most hits.csv figures are not re-pulled
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:189-198 (score recompute reads best_pchembl/qed/ro5_violations/similarity from the CSV row, not from a live fetch); only SMILES is re-fetched at cad/verify.py:158-180; hits.csv columns at cad/virtual_triage.py:197-199; overstated headlines at cad/verify.py:5-7, cad/verify.py:397, README.md:35 and README.md:135
verifies.correctedStatement: For each top ligand in hits.csv, verify.py re-pulls only the canonical SMILES live from ChEMBL (byte-equality); the potency (best_pchembl), drug-likeness (qed, ro5_violations) and similarity columns are read from the file and fed unchanged into the score recompute, while mw/alogp/hba/hbd/tpsa are not re-fetched or recomputed at all — so the recomputed score proves the column is internally consistent with its row inputs, not that those underlying figures are real (the live re-pull genuinely covers only the dossier's ChEMBL/UniProt counts and the per-ligand SMILES).
verifies.note: Confirmed but with one important caveat the audit understates: the dossier-level counts (ChEMBL potent-activity records, known-mechanism drug count, UniProt PDB count) ARE re-pulled live (verify.py:89-132), and SMILES is independently re-fetched, so it is not true that 'only ChEMBL/UniProt counts and SMILES' is the whole story for nothing-is-verified — rather, the inaccuracy is specifically the word 'every' in the README/docstring headlines. The verify.py docstring's own 'What this DOES and does NOT prove' section (lines 15-29) already discloses this nuance accurately; the overstatement lives only in the one-line headlines (verify.py:7, the runtime banner at verify.py:397, README:35, README:135).

verifies.title: SMILES byte-equal proves ID↔SMILES consistency, not that the molecule is a target hit
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:158-167 (reads row's chembl_id, fetches its canonical SMILES via _chembl_canonical_smiles(cid), compares to row's saved smiles); disclosure at docs/ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:53 ("never that a molecule works. Triage ≠ validation.") and README.md:145-147
verifies.correctedStatement: The verifier's SMILES check confirms only that each hit row's SMILES is byte-equal to ChEMBL's canonical record for that same row's ChEMBL ID — catching an edited, transposed, or invented structure string — but it does not establish that the listed compound is an actual potent ligand of the target, so a real ChEMBL molecule paired with its correct SMILES but wrong for the target would still pass.
verifies.note: The audit's technical description of verify.py is accurate (confirmed by cad/verify.py:155-180). However, the claim that this is a README inaccuracy is overstated: the repo never asserts the byte-equal check proves a molecule is a genuine target hit, and explicitly states the opposite limitation in two places (ANTI-HALLUCINATION.md:53 "never that a molecule works. Triage ≠ validation."; README:145-147 reproducibility-not-design-correctness). "Fabricated" in README:35/verify.py:171 means a fabricated SMILES string for an ID, which IS detected. So the limitation is real but already disclosed; the README is not overstating it. Spec mismatch = false is correct.

verifies.title: "byte-equal to ChEMBL" reads the same canonical_smiles field the pipeline read — not a fully orthogonal source
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:68-72 (returns molecule_structures.canonical_smiles via raw HTTP) vs cad/virtual_triage.py ("smiles": structs.get("canonical_smiles") in fetch_molecule_properties)
verifies.correctedStatement: The SMILES check re-fetches the same molecule_structures.canonical_smiles field from the same ChEMBL database over a separate raw-HTTP code path, so it proves hits.csv faithfully copied ChEMBL's canonical record (catching edited or transposed SMILES) but is not an independent measurement of the molecule from a different data source.
verifies.note: The audit's underlying fact is confirmed: verify.py:72 and virtual_triage.py both read molecule_structures.canonical_smiles from ChEMBL, so the check is byte-fidelity to ChEMBL's record, not an orthogonal structure measurement. But the audit's framing that the docs overstate independence is overstated itself: the docs already scope "independent" to mean a separate code path, not a separate source. verify.py:57-59 says it is "genuinely independent of how the run was produced"; verify.py:20-22 says it catches "an edited/transposed SMILES"; README:35 says "byte-equal to ChEMBL (raw HTTP, separate code path)." None claim an orthogonal data source. Hence partly: technically accurate observation, but it does not contradict the README as written.

verifies.title: "potent_activity_records" counts any pchembl-non-null activity, regardless of potency or assay type
verifies.evidence: cad/target_report.py:61 (query: only pchembl_value__isnull=false), :75 (field named "potent_activity_records"), :187 (printed as "Potent measured activities on record"); contrast cad/virtual_triage.py:45,139,145 (POTENCY_TYPES + min_pchembl default 6.0); cad/verify.py:16-19 ("A wrong-but-stable query would pass") and :108-111 (re-runs identical chembl_snapshot)
verifies.correctedStatement: The target dossier's "potent_activity_records" / "Potent measured activities on record" figure (cad/target_report.py:61,75,187) is just a raw ChEMBL count of every activity with a non-null pchembl_value for the target — it applies no minimum-pChEMBL threshold, no IC50/Ki/Kd/EC50 standard_type filter, and no assay-confidence filter, so it includes weak and non-binding-assay records and the word "potent" is unjustified; this is inconsistent with the repo's own triage stage (cad/virtual_triage.py), which does enforce min_pchembl and POTENCY_TYPES, and verify.py re-runs the identical query, so it confirms the number is reproducible but not that it actually measures potency.
verifies.note: Audit confirmed against the actual files. Minor nuance: a non-null pchembl_value is itself a mild implicit filter (ChEMBL only assigns one to -log-transformable concentration endpoints), and pChEMBL ~1 is rare rather than typical, so the "weak (pChEMBL ~1)" example is illustrative; the core defect — no potency threshold and no standard_type filter behind a "potent" label, contradicting the repo's own triage definition — is fully accurate. verify.py honestly self-discloses the reproducibility-vs-correctness gap at lines 16-19, so it does not silently give false confidence, but it does re-run the same mislabeled query.

verifies.title: provenance.py only validates the origin label string, not the value's true source
verifies.evidence: cad/provenance.py:93-98 (Figure.__post_init__ checks only that origin is in (FETCHED, COMPUTED)); cad/provenance.py:108-113 (Manifest.fetched/computed accept an arbitrary value:object with a caller-chosen label); README.md:34
verifies.correctedStatement: provenance.py enforces only that each figure's `origin` is the string `fetched` or `computed`, raising on anything else (including `model`); it does not verify that the recorded value actually came from the named source, so the no-model-figures rule depends on callers stamping honest labels rather than on a code-level guarantee.
verifies.note: The README's literal claim ("cannot record a value whose origin is 'model' — it raises", README.md:34) is technically TRUE: passing origin='model' does raise ValueError at cad/provenance.py:94-98. So the audit's framing ("does NOT make a model origin impossible") is partly overstated as a refutation of the literal text. However, the audit's core technical observation is correct and the README's surrounding guarantee ("Every reported figure is tagged fetched/computed, with its source") overstates enforcement strength: the check is a label-honesty check only. Nothing binds `value` (typed `object`) to the cited `source`, so a model-produced number passed via manifest.fetched(name, model_value, 'chembl', url) is accepted without error. Hence "partly": the literal sentence is defensible, but the implied guarantee is stronger than the code provides. Note also that the module docstring (cad/provenance.py:10-15) already concedes the guarantee is about honest labeling/re-verifiability, not runtime enforcement of value origin.

verifies.title: Manifest is never verified against the artifacts it describes
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:343-383 (run() reads dossier.json, hits.csv, liabilities.json, binding_site.json, cost_benefit.json but not provenance.json); verify.py:51 imports provenance only for source_url(); manifest written at cad/run_pipeline.py:165 -> cad/provenance.py:129
verifies.correctedStatement: verify.py re-pulls and recomputes the underlying figures directly from live sources and the run artifacts (dossier.json, hits.csv, cost_benefit.json, etc.), but it never reads provenance.json, so the manifest's recorded values and origins are not cross-checked against the artifacts and a manifest edited in isolation would not trigger a FAIL.
verifies.note: Real limitation but do not overstate: the same figures ARE independently re-verified by verify.py from live sources plus deterministic recompute, and cad/provenance.py raises at write time if a figure's origin would be "model". The gap is narrowly that the provenance.json file itself sits outside verify's reproof loop.

verifies.title: cost-benefit "reproduces exactly" only validates internal consistency, not the inputs or priors
verifies.evidence: cad/verify.py:216-221 (recomputes from saved assumptions block) and cad/verify.py:235-237 (prints "all figures reproduce exactly from stated assumptions" PASS); cad/cost_benefit.py:36-41 and :54,:68 (LOA priors and oncology factor are hardcoded heuristics, none fetched from the cited DOI); cad/run_pipeline.py:159-161 (cites the source URL only as a label).
verifies.correctedStatement: The cost-benefit PASS only confirms the saved outputs are a deterministic recomputation of the saved assumptions (a tamper check); it does not verify those assumptions (incidence, price, penetration, years) nor that the hardcoded per-phase success priors in cad/cost_benefit.py match the cited BIO/Informa source, so fabricated-but-self-consistent inputs still pass.
verifies.note: Audit confirmed against actual files (located under cad/, not repo root). The verify step re-runs cost_benefit.analyze() on the saved assumptions and compares outputs; the priors LOA_FROM_PHASE (cost_benefit.py:36-41) and ONCOLOGY_LOA_FACTOR (:68) are hardcoded literals explicitly commented as "rough"/"HEURISTIC, not fetched data"/"editable", and prov.source_url(...) supplies only a citation string, never a fetched value to check against. No spec mismatch.

verifies.title: Only the first 5 hits (and 25 liabilities) are verified
verifies.correctedStatement: The independent verifier only re-checks the top 5 ranked ligands in hits.csv (SMILES byte-equality vs ChEMBL and triage-score recompute) and the first 25 entries in liabilities.json (PAINS/Brenk recompute); any rows beyond those fixed caps are never verified, so a fabricated or edited row past the cap would pass unnoticed.
verifies.note: Confirmed against the real file. The audit cited the path as bare verify.py; the actual file is cad/verify.py and the line numbers match exactly. Both verify functions slice their input to the default max_rows (5 for hits, 25 for liabilities), and the only callers (lines 362 and 366) do not override the defaults, so the caps are in force in practice. The caps are parameterizable defaults, not hard-coded constants, but nothing raises them.

verifies.title: 'clean' label ignores Brenk structural alerts
verifies.evidence: cad/cheminformatics.py:113; web/public/data/cheminformatics/BRAF.json:9-21
verifies.correctedStatement: The `clean` flag means a compound passes Lipinski Ro5, Veber, and has zero PAINS alerts only; Brenk structural alerts are computed and reported but excluded from the flag, so a compound with Brenk alerts can still be labeled clean (175 committed entries are clean:true with brenkAlerts>0, e.g. BRAF CHEMBL500659 with brenkAlerts=3).
verifies.note: Verified directly. Line 113 defines clean = ro5 and veber and len(pains_hits) == 0; brenk_hits are calculated (line 98) and emitted as brenk_alerts (line 111) but never gate clean. The module docstring (line 10) advertises 'structural-alert screening (PAINS, Brenk)'. A script over all web/public/data/cheminformatics/*.json found 175 entries with brenkAlerts>0 and clean:true, corroborating the cited BRAF and ERBB2 examples.

verifies.title: SKIP/unresolved paths convert un-checkable figures into a non-FAIL
verifies.evidence: verify.py:252-253 (binding box: any fetch/parse Exception -> SKIP), verify.py:279-282 (liabilities: RDKit absent -> SKIP), verify.py:385 & 394 (n_fail counts only FAIL; SKIP excluded; exit 1 only if n_fail), verify.py:173-180 (SMILES: total unresolve -> FAIL, partial -> PASS with silent "(N unresolved, skipped)"), verify.py:80 (count checks: live is None -> FAIL)
verifies.correctedStatement: The two deterministic recompute checks — liabilities.json (when RDKit is absent, verify.py:279-282) and the docking box (on any RCSB fetch/parse error, verify.py:252-253) — emit SKIP rather than FAIL, and because SKIP triggers neither the non-zero exit code nor a qualified banner (verify.py:385,394,415), an edited liabilities.json or binding_site.json can pass unverified in an environment lacking RDKit or with the PDB fetch blocked; the ChEMBL/UniProt count checks (verify.py:80) and the SMILES identity check (verify.py:173-175) do fail closed (FAIL) on total source unavailability, so the gap is limited to those two deterministic checks plus individually-unresolved SMILES rows that are silently dropped within an otherwise-PASS.
verifies.note: Core claim is real for the two deterministic recompute checks: missing RDKit or a blocked PDB fetch downgrades a genuine integrity check to SKIP, which does not count toward n_fail (line 385/394) and does not qualify the "Every reported figure reproduced exactly" banner (line 415) — only the no-target-evidence case (408-411) qualifies the banner. However the audit overstates: (1) the SMILES check (cited 173-178) does NOT fail open — total unresolution FAILs (line 173-175) and only a specifically-unresolvable individual row is silently skipped while the check still PASSes; an attacker would need to make exactly the edited row's ChEMBL fetch fail while others succeed, a much narrower attack than "make the network fail." (2) The ChEMBL/UniProt count checks fail closed (line 80, FAIL when live is None), so "downgrades real checks to SKIP" does not apply to the whole suite — only the liabilities and binding-box recompute checks genuinely fail open.

verifies.title: Veber rule omits the HBD+HBA<=12 alternative clause
verifies.evidence: cad/cheminformatics.py:91 (veber = rotb <= 10 and tpsa <= 140), exposed as "veber_ok" at line 108; hbd/hba computed at lines 80-81 but unused in the rule.
verifies.correctedStatement: The veber_ok flag implements only the TPSA branch of the Veber 2002 oral-bioavailability rule (rotatable bonds <=10 and TPSA <=140) and omits the alternative H-bond clause (HBD+HBA <=12), so a molecule with TPSA >140 but <=12 total H-bond donors+acceptors is reported as failing Veber when the full rule would pass it.
verifies.note: Real, accurately described inaccuracy. It is a widely used simplification (PSA-only Veber), but the original paper does offer the HBD+HBA<=12 alternative, and the code has hbd/hba in hand yet does not use them, so molecules with high TPSA but low H-bond count are misclassified as fails.

verifies.title: Egan rule is a rectangular approximation of an ellipsoidal absorption model
verifies.evidence: cad/cheminformatics.py:92 (egan = tpsa <= 131.6 and logp <= 5.88); logp source at cad/cheminformatics.py:78 (Crippen.MolLogP); tpsa at line 79 (CalcTPSA)
verifies.correctedStatement: The egan_ok flag is a rectangular approximation of the Egan/Merz 2000 absorption model: instead of the original confidence ellipse in PSA vs. AlogP98 space, it applies two independent axis-bound cutoffs (TPSA <= 131.6 and MolLogP <= 5.88) using RDKit's Crippen MolLogP as a stand-in for AlogP98, so compounds near the ellipse corners can be classified differently than the true Egan "egg".
verifies.note: Confirmed against the actual file. Line 92 uses a simple AND of two thresholds (a bounding box), not the elliptical boundary defined by Egan, Merz & Baldwin (J Med Chem 2000). The 131.6 / 5.88 values correspond to the egg's axis extremes, and the code feeds Crippen MolLogP (line 78) where the published model uses AlogP98, so descriptor and geometry both differ from the source. Practically the deviation is limited to a thin region near the ellipse corners; the rectangle is permissive (any point inside the ellipse is also inside the box, but the corners of the box are outside the ellipse), so the only misclassifications are false-positives near the corners, which keeps the statement from being overstated.

verifies.title: PAINS/Brenk arrays truncated to 5 while count is full
verifies.evidence: cad/cheminformatics.py:110-111 ("pains_alerts": len(pains_hits), "pains": pains_hits[:5]; "brenk_alerts": len(brenk_hits), "brenk": brenk_hits[:5]) and cad/precompute_site_data.py:102-103 ("painsAlerts": a["pains_alerts"], "pains": a["pains"]; "brenkAlerts": a["brenk_alerts"], "brenk": a["brenk"])
verifies.correctedStatement: The JSON pains and brenk arrays are capped at the first 5 matched structural alerts (pains_hits[:5] / brenk_hits[:5]), so for a compound with more than 5 alerts the listed names are fewer than the true total reported in painsAlerts/brenkAlerts.
verifies.note: Verified directly in both cited files. In cheminformatics.py the analyze() return slices the hit lists to [:5] while the *_alerts fields store the full len(); precompute_site_data.py passes these through unchanged into painsAlerts/pains and brenkAlerts/brenk. The count fields are the full true count and the list fields are truncated, exactly as the audit claims. Not a spec mismatch — no doc promises a complete list — just a silent truncation that can make the array shorter than the count.

verifies.title: "Best" PDB ignores sequence coverage / which domain is resolved
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:65-72 (props parsed from xref keep only Method/Resolution, no "Chains"/coverage range), and cad/fetch_structure.py:102-105 (rank = (method-preference tier, resolution) only); docstrings at lines 9-11 and 99 call this the "best experimental structure".
verifies.correctedStatement: The structure picker selects a PDB entry solely by experimental method (X-ray/EM preferred) and resolution, ignoring the UniProt-reported chain coverage range, so it can return a high-resolution structure of an isolated domain or short fragment that omits the druggable site rather than a more complete or more relevant structure.
verifies.note: Verified directly in the file. resolve_uniprot (lines 62-72) constructs props from the PDB cross-reference's properties but only captures Method and Resolution; the UniProt PDB xref also carries a "Chains" property encoding the resolved residue range, which is never read. pick_best_pdb (lines 98-105) ranks by (method tier, _res_num) with no coverage or length term. The same omission is duplicated in the --uniprot branch (lines 135-145). SPEC MISMATCH = false is correct; this is a design limitation, not a contradiction of a stated spec — the docstrings honestly disclose the criterion is "best resolution", but the word "best experimental structure" overstates suitability for docking.

verifies.title: Committed JSON is a non-reproducible live-ChEMBL snapshot; precompute can serve stale per-target data while index date advances
verifies.evidence: cad/precompute_site_data.py:88-89,130,158 and cad/virtual_triage.py:60-62
verifies.correctedStatement: The committed cheminformatics JSON is not a reproducible artifact: although each molecule's descriptors are deterministic from its SMILES, the compound set is pulled live (uncached, with a wall-clock budget that can truncate results) from ChEMBL by virtual_triage.py, so re-running on a later date can return different molecules; and because a target whose triage fails is skipped via `continue` (leaving its old per-target file in place) while the merge-by-slug guard keeps its stale index entry, index.json's top-level `generated` stamp is rewritten to the new run's date and can therefore advertise a freshness that some per-target files do not actually have.
verifies.note: Fully confirmed by reading both files. (1) Live, uncached ChEMBL query: virtual_triage.py:60-62 uses urllib.request.urlopen against https://www.ebi.ac.uk/chembl/api/data with no caching, and lines 108-120 cap the scan with a time budget that can yield partial/varying results even against unchanged data. (2) Failure path leaves stale per-target file: precompute_site_data.py:88-89 `if not data: continue` skips the write at :134, so the previously committed <TARGET>.json (carrying its own older "generated" stamp from :130) remains. (3) Never-shrink index merge: :147-156 merges existing index entries by slug, preserving the stale target's entry. (4) Index date advances regardless: :158 writes {"generated": args.stamp, ...}, so the top-level date is always the current run's stamp even when underlying per-target files were not refreshed. The only minor caveat is that args.stamp (:70) is operator-supplied to avoid nondeterministic clocks, but that controls the recorded date, not the compound-set reproducibility, so it does not weaken the claim.

verifies.title: AlphaFold pLDDT confidence is claimed-needed but never read
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:158-171 (AlphaFold fallback downloads + reports unconditionally); cad/fetch_structure.py:170 is the sole occurrence of "pLDDT", a literal note string; grep across cad/ finds no pLDDT/B-factor parsing.
verifies.correctedStatement: When no experimental PDB exists, fetch_structure.py downloads the AlphaFold predicted model and reports it identically regardless of quality; it never parses or thresholds the per-residue pLDDT confidence, so the "validate confidence (pLDDT) before docking" note is purely an advisory string left for the human to act on, not an automated check.
verifies.note: Confirmed. The AlphaFold branch calls _download then _report with a static note; no code reads the B-factor/pLDDT column or conditions the docking handoff on it. The note is honest advice rather than an implemented gate, so a low-confidence model is treated the same as a high-confidence one.

verifies.title: saScore would silently vanish on refresh in a Contrib-less RDKit, changing JSON schema
verifies.evidence: cad/precompute_site_data.py:111 (entry["saScore"] added only if a.get("sa_score") is not None); cad/cheminformatics.py:54-56 (_HAS_SASCORE=False on import failure) and :100 (sa_score=None when not _HAS_SASCORE); all 38 per-compound files in web/public/data/cheminformatics/*.json contain saScore (only index.json, an index, lacks it).
verifies.correctedStatement: The per-compound saScore field is emitted only when RDKit's optional Contrib SA_Score tree is installed; regenerating the cheminformatics data on an RDKit build that lacks it would silently omit saScore from every entry, producing a schema that differs from the 38 committed data files which all include it.
verifies.note: Real and accurately described. saScore is conditionally added (precompute_site_data.py:111), and sa_score falls back to None when the SA_Score Contrib import fails (cheminformatics.py:54-56,100). The omission is silent (no error, field dropped rather than set to null). All 38 actual data files carry saScore; index.json is just an index and is not a counterexample.

verifies.title: No apo/holo, mutant, or construct filtering — can select an unsuitable structure
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:98-105 (pick_best_pdb ranks only on method preference + resolution; resolve_uniprot at :56 fetches only accession,xref_pdb so no mutation/ligand data is consulted); cad/binding_site.py:53-76 (largest_ligand is a HETATM-vs-IGNORE-set heuristic) and :100-103 (apo case returns an error rather than a box)
verifies.correctedStatement: Structure selection chooses the best PDB purely by experimental method and resolution, with no check of mutation status (e.g. EGFR T790M or kinase-dead variants), apo/holo state, or chimeric/engineered constructs, so a catalytically-dead or non-physiological structure can be picked; binding_site.py then errors out on a truly apo entry but can still draw a box around a non-solvent additive not in its fixed IGNORE list.
verifies.note: Confirmed as a real limitation. Minor overstatement in the audit: binding_site.py does not silently "derive no box" for apo structures — largest_ligand returns None and the tool prints "no non-solvent ligand found (apo structure?)" and exits non-zero (binding_site.py:100-103). The "box around a crystallisation additive" only occurs when an additive is absent from the hard-coded IGNORE set (lines 31-35) and is the largest hetero group. The mutant/construct selection gap is entirely unmitigated. Note the README/docstring claim is a paraphrase: the actual docstring (fetch_structure.py:5-11) promises coordinates for downstream work, not an explicit suitability guarantee.

verifies.title: Gene→UniProt resolution takes top relevance hit (size=1) — can fetch the wrong protein
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:55-60 — q = f"(gene:{name} OR protein_name:{name}) AND organism_id:9606 AND reviewed:true"; urlencode(... 'size': 1 ...); results = _get_json(url).get("results", []); e = results[0]
verifies.correctedStatement: resolve_uniprot() resolves a gene/target name by submitting a fuzzy UniProt search over both the gene and protein_name fields, requesting only the single top-ranked reviewed human hit (size=1) and using results[0] with no check that the returned entry's gene symbol exactly matches the input, so an ambiguous, short, or substring-matching name can silently resolve to the wrong protein and propagate that error through the entire structure/box/dock chain.
verifies.note: Verified by opening the file directly. The query uses UniProt's gene:/protein_name: token search (not exact-match operators like gene_exact:), size is hard-coded to 1, and results[0] is consumed unconditionally — there is no post-filter comparing the entry's gene name to the requested symbol. The audit's characterization is accurate, including the absence of exact-symbol verification and the downstream propagation risk. SPEC MISMATCH = false is also correct: the docstring on line 53-54 promises 'a reviewed human UniProt entry' which it does return; the issue is correctness/precision of which entry, not a contradiction with the stated spec.

verifies.title: Blind-box fallback uses `--autobox`, unsupported by upstream AutoDock Vina
verifies.evidence: cad/dock.py:163 (cmd += ["--autobox"]) in the no-center branch (lines 160-163); install target is ccsb-scripps/AutoDock-Vina at cad/dock.py:46; docstring promises blind box at cad/dock.py:14-16
verifies.correctedStatement: The "omit --center for a blind box" fallback (cad/dock.py:160-163) invokes `vina ... --autobox`, but upstream AutoDock Vina (the ccsb-scripps build the wrapper tells users to install) has no `--autobox` option, so this path errors out instead of performing blind docking; an explicit --center/--size (or --box-json) box is effectively required.
verifies.note: Real spec mismatch. Upstream AutoDock Vina accepts center_x/y/z + size_x/y/z (or a --config file) and exposes no auto-box flag; automatic box sizing is a smina/gnina feature (and even there the flag is `--autobox_ligand`, not bare `--autobox`). Since cad/dock.py:40-46 directs users to conda `vina` or the ccsb-scripps release binary, the no-center branch will fail with an unrecognized-option/usage error at line 167-168 rather than docking over the whole receptor. The fix is to compute a center/size from the receptor's coordinates (or require --center) instead of passing --autobox.

verifies.title: Pipeline labels docking 'feasible' from PDB count alone
verifies.evidence: cad/run_pipeline.py:175 ("docking feasible: {'yes' if u.get('pdb_count') else 'no'}"); pdb_count defined as len(all UniProt PDB cross-references) at cad/target_report.py:93,99; same truthiness logic at cad/target_report.py:174
verifies.correctedStatement: The SUMMARY/dossier "docking feasible: yes/no" flag is set solely by whether the target's UniProt record lists at least one PDB cross-reference (pdb_count > 0); it does not verify that any structure is holo, covers the relevant domain/binding site, or has a definable pocket, so a target with only an apo or wrong-domain PDB is still reported as "docking feasible: yes".
verifies.note: Verified against the actual files, not the audit. cad/target_report.py:93 collects every uniProtKBCrossReferences entry with database=="PDB" and stores the raw count as pdb_count (line 99). run_pipeline.py:175 emits "yes" whenever that count is truthy. There is no holo/apo, domain-coverage, or pocket-detection check anywhere in this decision; the binding-box/pocket work in cad/binding_site.py is a separate downstream step and does not gate this label. Audit is accurate; spec-mismatch is correctly marked false (this is an accuracy/overstatement issue, not a documented-spec deviation).

verifies.title: AlphaFold fetch only ever gets fragment F1 — truncates large proteins
verifies.evidence: cad/fetch_structure.py:88 (probe URL hardcoded `AF-{acc}-F1-model_v{v}.pdb`), :162 (output filename hardcoded `AF-{acc}-F1.pdb`), :83-84 (API path returns only `data[0]["pdbUrl"]`, the first fragment)
verifies.correctedStatement: AlphaFold-fallback structures are limited to the first fragment: the probe URL and output filename are hardcoded to "-F1" and the API path uses only the first prediction entry (data[0]), so for proteins longer than AlphaFold's ~2,700-residue per-model limit (which AlphaFold DB splits into F1, F2, … fragments) only the F1 fragment is downloaded, silently yielding a partial structure for very large targets.
verifies.note: Verified against the actual file. The core inaccuracy is real and present in all three AlphaFold paths — only the first fragment is ever fetched, and the saved file is always named F1 regardless of source. Minor imprecision in the audit: the API-derived URL (line 84) is not literally hardcoded to '-F1-' (it is taken from the API's pdbUrl), but since the code reads only data[0] it still resolves to the first fragment, so the truncation conclusion stands. Note this only affects the AlphaFold fallback path; targets resolved to an experimental RCSB PDB entry are unaffected.
